inputs: $a^n\pm b^n$ modulo primes and prime squares
technique(s).purpose: rewrite divisibility as congruences for $g\equiv ab^{-1}$

technique(s).name: order_arguments
technique(s).purpose: convert $g^n\equiv 1$ into divisibility constraints on $n$

technique(s).name: prime_power_order_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift multiplicative order from $r$ to $r^2$

technique(s).name: square_root_uniqueness_mod_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: identify the unique nontrivial square root of $1$ as $-1$

technique(s).name: LTE_valuation
technique(s).purpose: translate exact valuation into an exact constraint on $v_p(n)$

technique(s).name: digit_sum_and_carry_invariant
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)$ via digit sums / carries

technique(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: multiple congruence and divisibility constraints on $n$
technique(s).purpose: solve coupled congruences and parametrize solutions as an arithmetic progression

theorem(s).name: Fermat_little_theorem
theorem(s).role: Used to certify $4^{11}\equiv 1\pmod{23}$ and bound $\operatorname{ord}_{23}(4)$.

theorem(s).name: order_lifting_prime_to_square
theorem(s).role: If $\operatorname{ord}_r(g)=d$ and $g^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$ then $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(g)=dr$.

theorem(s).name: square_roots_of_one_mod_prime_power
theorem(s).role: For odd prime $r$, $x^2\equiv 1\pmod{r^k}$ implies $x\equiv \pm 1\pmod{r^k}$.

theorem(s).name: LTE
theorem(s).role: If $p\mid(a-b)$ and $p\nmid ab$, then $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+v_p(n)$.

theorem(s).name: Legendre_formula
theorem(s).role: Gives $v_p(m!)$ and implies a digit-sum expression for $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)$.

theorem(s).name: digit_sum_carry_identity
theorem(s).role: Relates digit sums to number of carries in base-$p$ addition.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Unit groups modulo prime powers and digit-carry invariants jointly control admissible exponents.

critical_trick(s): Use prime-square order lifting to turn $r^2\mid(a^n+b^n)$ into a forced half-order congruence for $n$, combine with $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ by CRT, enforce an exact $p$-adic depth via LTE, then use digit carries to force $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)=c$ and select the unique smallest progression element.

title: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer) -- Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer) -- Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $n$ be a positive integer whose base-$p$ expansion ends with a block of $t\ge1$ digits equal to $p-1$, followed by a digit $a\in\{0,1,\dots,p-2\}$, followed by a block of $s\ge1$ zeros, and then a nonzero digit $b\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}$. Consider the sequence $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$ for $0\le k\le n$. Define $k^*$ to be the smallest index $1\le k\le n-1$ for which $p\mid A_k$, and put $e=v_p(A_{k^*})$.
givens: $p\ge5$ is an odd prime
$t\ge1$ and $s\ge1$ are integers
$u\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge0}$

Let $n=u\,p^{t+s+2}+b\,p^{t+s+1}+a\,p^{t}+(p^{t}-1)$ (equivalently, the base-$p$ digits of $n$ are $(p-1,\dots,p-1,a,\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{s\text{ zeros}},b,\text{digits of }u)$ from least to most significant).
For $0\le k\le n$, define $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$. Let $k^*=\min\{1\le k\le n-1: p\mid A_k\}$ and $e=v_p(A_{k^*})$.
ask: (i) Prove that $k^*$ exists and is uniquely determined by the above digit pattern of $n$, and show that in fact $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$.
(ii) Compute $N\equiv k^*+p^{e}\pmod{1000}$, and report the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base for Lucas/Kummer; determines the digit system and the $p$-adic valuation

parameter(s).name: t
parameter(s).purpose: length of the trailing $(p-1)$-digit block in the base-$p$ expansion of $n$; forces the location of the first Lucas-vanishing digit

parameter(s).name: s
parameter(s).purpose: length of the zero block after digit $a$; forces a nontrivial carry chain of length $s+1$ in Kummer carry counting

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: the first base-$p$ digit of $n$ (from the least significant side) that is not equal to $p-1$

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: a nonzero base-$p$ digit above the zero block; ensures $k^*<n$ and terminates the carry chain

parameter(s).name: u
parameter(s).purpose: higher base-$p$ digits of $n$ (above the controlled suffix); allows structural variation without changing the suffix interface

invariant(s).name: structured_base_p_suffix
invariant(s).statement: The integer $n=u\,p^{t+s+2}+b\,p^{t+s+1}+a\,p^{t}+(p^{t}-1)$ has base-$p$ suffix $(\underbrace{p-1,\dots,p-1}_{t\text{ digits}},a,\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{s\text{ zeros}},b)$ (least to most significant within the suffix), with $a\le p-2$ and $b\ge1$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guards_1
invariant(s).statement: Non-degeneracy: $p\ge5$, $t\ge1$, and $s\ge1$, so the row index $n\ge p^{t+s+1}$ and the carry count in the valuation step is at least $2$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guards_2
invariant(s).statement: Non-degeneracy: $a\in\{0,1,\dots,p-2\}$ (so the first base-$p$ digit of $n$ that is not $p-1$ occurs at position $t$) and $b\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}$ (so $k^*<n$ and the carry chain terminates).

invariant(s).name: Lucas_nonvanishing_criterion
invariant(s).statement: For $0\le k\le n$, Lucas' theorem implies $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ iff every base-$p$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding digit of $n$.

invariant(s).name: Kummer_carry_valuation
invariant(s).statement: Kummer's carry interpretation: for $0\le k\le n$, $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$.

invariant(s).name: forced_carry_chain_for_kstar
invariant(s).statement: For $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$, subtracting $k^*$ from $n$ triggers a borrow at digit-position $t$ that propagates through exactly the $s$ consecutive zero digits above it and stops at the digit $b\ge1$; hence the base-$p$ addition $k^*+(n-k^*)$ produces exactly $s+1$ carries.

essential_assumption(s): Digits are taken in base $p$; the instance must keep $p$ prime to use Lucas/Kummer in their standard forms.

The index $k^*$ is defined implicitly (as a minimality object); solving an instance requires first using Lucas' digitwise nonvanishing criterion to determine $k^*$, and only then using a $p$-adic valuation method (Kummer carries / Legendre) to determine $e$.
The reported output is the canonical representative of an integer modulo $1000$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$; intermediate quantities may be larger but must be reduced deterministically at the end.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem makes the predicate $p\mid\binom{n}{k}$ equivalent to a base-$p$ digit inequality, so the least such $k^*$ is forced by the controlled suffix of $n$ (hence existence and uniqueness of $k^*$ under the guards).
Kummer's carry theorem then forces $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k^*}\right)$ as an exact carry count determined by the same digit pattern, so $N\equiv k^*+p^e\pmod{1000}$ is uniquely determined.
Normalization: reduce modulo $1000$ and select the canonical representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: Lucas_theorem
inputs: base-$p$ digits of $n$ and a variable index $k$
technique(s).purpose: Characterize when $\binom{n}{k}\equiv0\pmod p$ and locate the first index $k^*$ where this vanishing occurs under the structured digit suffix.
outputs: closed-form identification of the minimal $k^*$ from digit constraints

technique(s).name: v_p_via_Kummer_carries
technique(s).purpose: Compute $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k^*}\right)$ by counting carries.
outputs: exact value of $e$ as a carry count

technique(s).name: Lucas_Kummer_interface_step
technique(s).purpose: Use Lucas to pin down $k^*$ via the first digit where $k$ must exceed the corresponding digit of $n$, then use Kummer carry counting on that specific $k^*$ to obtain $e$ for the modulus-$1000$ computation.
outputs: two-phase pipeline $(n\text{ digits})\Rightarrow k^*\Rightarrow e$ that determines $N$

technique(s).name: mod_1000_reduction
technique(s).purpose: Compute $k^*+p^e$ modulo $1000$.

theorem(s).name: combinatorial_count_as_integer
theorem(s).role: justify integrality of binomial coefficients

theorem(s).name: Lucas_theorem
theorem(s).role: digitwise evaluation of binomial coefficients modulo a prime

theorem(s).name: Kummer_theorem
theorem(s).role: interprets $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ as the number of carries in base $p$

theorem(s).name: Legendre_formula
theorem(s).role: factorial-valuation computation to cross-check $v_p\binom{n}{k}$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Base-$p$ expansions control both $\binom{n}{k}\bmod p$ (Lucas) and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Kummer/Legendre).

critical_trick(s): Use the shared base-$p$ digit interface: Lucas forces the first vanishing coefficient index $k^*$, then Kummer/Legendre forces $e$.
Exploit the engineered suffix of $n$: the block of $s$ zeros forces a carry chain of length $s+1$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Base conversion, carry counting, and modular arithmetic with exact integers.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Verify the base-$17$ digit vector of $n$ matches the declared suffix.
- Compute $k^*$ using Lucas' digit-inequality criterion and confirm that $k^*-1$ is nonvanishing mod $17$ while $k^*$ is vanishing.
- Compute $e$ by Kummer carry counting, and compute $N\equiv k^*+17^e\pmod{1000}$.

NT5 preflight attempts:
- Small-mod-only probes with moduli in {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11} produce very small first-zero indices unrelated to $k^*$.
- A bounded search up to $2\times 10^5$ for $k$ with $17\mid\binom{n}{k}$ finds none (the true $k^*$ is about $1.7\times10^7$).
- Template guesses such as $N\equiv n\pmod{1000}$, $N\equiv k^*\pmod{1000}$, or $N\equiv 17^e\pmod{1000}$ fail.

NT6 collision:
- A plausible wrong-branch guess $k=a\,17^t$ (using $a=11$) gives a different normalized residue modulo $1000$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent route:
- Determine $k^*$ by scanning the base-$17$ digits of $n$ for the first digit $<16$ and applying the explicit formula $k^*=(n_{i_0}+1)17^{i_0}$.
- Compute $e$ via Legendre's digit-sum formula for $v_{17}\binom{n}{k^*}$, and recompute $N$.

verification_notes: The smallest forbidden Lucas digit occurs at position 5, giving k*=17038284.
The base-17 borrow chain has length e=7, so k*+17^e mod 1000 = 957.

title: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer)

identity.short_name: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer)

problem_spec.summary: Concrete instance (p,n,k fixed) of the Lucas/Legendre/Kummer binomial family: compute e=v_p(C(n,k)) and r\equivC(n,k) (mod p), then output the evaluation integer N with 0\leqN<1000 and N\equiv271e+r (mod 1000).
givens: $p=13$ is prime
$0\le k\le n$
ask: Let $e=v_{13}\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ and let $r$ be the unique integer with $0\le r<13$ and $\binom{n}{k}\equiv r\pmod{13}$.
Define $N$ to be the unique integer with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv 271e+r\pmod{1000}$. Output $N$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: sets the base for digit expansions and the valuation

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: top index of the binomial coefficient

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: bottom index; controls carry structure in base $p$

parameter(s).name: variant
parameter(s).purpose: extends to related combinatorial divisibility tasks while keeping the same base-$p$ interface

invariant(s).name: base_p_digit_interface
invariant(s).statement: Write $n=\sum n_i p^i$ and $k=\sum k_i p^i$ with digits $0\le n_i,k_i<p$. The digit data $(n_i,k_i)$ drive both Lucas (mod $p$ residue) and Kummer/Legendre (valuation via carries).

invariant(s).name: carry_count_equals_vp
invariant(s).statement: (Optional/Kummer) $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$.

essential_assumption(s): Digits are taken in base $p$; the instance must keep $p$ prime to use Lucas/Kummer in their standard forms.
When outputting a residue $r$, normalization is $0\le r<p$ (structural).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Both $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ and the reduced residue $r\in\{0,\dots,p-1\}$ are uniquely determined by $n,k,p$; thus the pair $(e,r)$ is globally unique.
The mapping $N\equiv 271e+r\pmod{1000}$ with $0\le N<1000$ yields a unique integer output.

technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p(n!)$ via floor-sum

technique(s).name: vp_binomial
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ by subtracting factorial valuations

technique(s).name: Lucas_theorem
technique(s).purpose: compute $\binom{n}{k}\bmod p$ digitwise

technique(s).name: Kummer_theorem_optional
technique(s).purpose: (optional) compute $e$ via carry counting rather than floor sums
outputs: interpretation of $e$ as number of carries

theorem(s).name: combinatorial_count_as_integer
theorem(s).role: justify integrality of expressions by counting interpretations (often used to prove divisibility)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Base-$p$ expansions expose how factorials accumulate prime powers and how binomial coefficients decompose digitwise modulo $p$.

critical_trick(s): Use the shared interface "base-$p$ digits of $n$ and $k$": Lucas gives the mod $p$ residue and Kummer/Legendre gives the $p$-adic valuation.
For sum-of-binomial divisibility variants, interpret the sum as counting objects modulo $p$ or track valuation term-by-term.

title: Digit-box moments for indices with $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ (Lucas + factorized second moment)

identity.short_name: Digit-box moments for indices with $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ (Lucas + factorized second moment)

problem_spec.summary: For a prime $p$ and an integer $n$ specified by a fixed base-$p$ digit vector (with at least one controlled zero digit), use Lucas' theorem to identify the set of indices $k$ for which $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv0\pmod p$ as a Cartesian digit-box. Compute both the size of this set and the sum of squares of its elements, and output their sum modulo $1000$.
givens: $p\ge5$ is an odd prime
$d_0,d_1,d_2,d_3,d_4,d_5\in\{0,1,\dots,p-1\}$ with a guard that at least one $d_i\ne p-1$
Define $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ (i.e., the base-$p$ digits of $n$ are $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$ from least to most significant)
For $0\le k\le n$, define $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$
Define $S=\{k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}: p\nmid A_k\}$, $C=|S|$, and $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$
ask: (i) Using Lucas' theorem, show that $S$ is exactly the set of $k$ whose base-$p$ digits $(k_0,\dots,k_5)$ satisfy $0\le k_i\le d_i$, and deduce $C=\prod_{i=0}^5(d_i+1)$.
(ii) Compute $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ by digit-box moment factorization and report the normalized integer $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv C+Q\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: modulus/base for Lucas' theorem and for the base-$p$ digit system

parameter(s).name: d_0
parameter(s).purpose: least significant base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_1
parameter(s).purpose: second base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_2
parameter(s).purpose: third base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_3
parameter(s).purpose: fourth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_4
parameter(s).purpose: fifth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_5
parameter(s).purpose: sixth base-$p$ digit of $n$; ensures a fixed 6-digit digit-box interface

invariant(s).name: fixed_six_digit_base_p_n
invariant(s).statement: The integer $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ has base-$p$ digits $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$ (least to most significant).

invariant(s).name: Lucas_nonvanishing_digit_criterion
invariant(s).statement: For $0\le k\le n$, Lucas' theorem implies $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ iff every base-$p$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding digit of $n$.

invariant(s).name: digit_box_model_for_S
invariant(s).statement: The set $S=\{k: p\nmid \binom{n}{k}\}$ is in bijection with the Cartesian product $\prod_{i=0}^{5}\{0,\dots,d_i\}$ (a digit-box).

invariant(s).name: digit_box_second_moment_factorization
invariant(s).statement: The second moment $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ can be computed by expanding $k^2$ in base $p$ digits and factoring the resulting sums over the digit-box into one-digit moments.

essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime so Lucas' theorem applies.
Binomial coefficients are integers, so divisibility by $p$ is well-defined.
Normalization: reduce $C+Q$ modulo $1000$ and take the canonical representative in $\{0,\dots,999\}$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem forces $S$ exactly as a digitwise-inequality set, hence $S$ (and thus $C$) is unique.
The digit-box structure and digit-box factorization force the moment computation for $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$, hence $Q$ is unique.
Normalization modulo $1000$ selects a unique $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: base_p_digit_decomposition
inputs: explicit expression for $n$ in powers of $p$
technique(s).purpose: Extract the base-$p$ digit vector of $n$ needed for Lucas' digitwise criterion.

technique(s).name: Lucas_theorem
inputs: base-$p$ digits of $n$ and a variable index $k$
technique(s).purpose: Characterize $S=\{k: p\nmid \binom{n}{k}\}$ by digit inequalities.

technique(s).name: digit_box_bijection

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Base-$p$ expansions convert a modular binomial constraint into digitwise independent constraints, enabling separable enumeration and moment computation.

critical_trick(s): Replace $\{k: p\nmid\binom{n}{k}\}$ by a digit-box via Lucas, then compute $Q=\sum k^2$ by digit-box moment-factorization of the square expansion.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Confirm the base-$19$ digit vector of $n$ matches the declared digits.
- Using Lucas' digitwise inequality criterion, compute $C=|S|$ and compute $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ via the digit-box moment-factorization formula.
- Reduce $C+Q$ modulo $1000$ to obtain the normalized $N$.

Preflight attempts:
- 'Attempt A: naive guess $N_{\text{guess}}\equiv n+C\pmod{1000}$; verify it differs from the true $N$.'
- 'Attempt B: bounded scan $k\in[0,50000]$ using the Lucas digit test; compute $(C_{\le B}+Q_{\le B})\bmod 1000$ and confirm it differs from the true $N$.'
- 'Attempt C: compute only $(C+Q)\bmod 8$ and observe this leaves multiple candidates in $\{0,\dots,999\}$.'

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent route:
- Enumerate the digit-box $\prod_i\{0,\dots,d_i\}$ directly, map each digit tuple to $k=\sum k_i19^i$, and compute $C$ and $Q$ from the explicit list.
- Compare the resulting $N\bmod 1000$ with the primary computation.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalize the parent-instance and daughter-instance defining $n$ expressions as polynomials in a formal variable $p$ using sympy (expand + simplify + together + factor + Poly).
Normalize by dividing by gcd of integer coefficients and by fixing the leading coefficient sign.
Try the deterministic transforms:
- identity renaming $p\mapsto p$
- monomial shift $f(p)\mapsto p^{k}f(p)$ with rational scaling chosen to match leading terms (if degrees allow)
Confirm the canonical forms do not match under these transforms.

verification_notes: Enumerated the Lucas-allowed digit box with 21600 indices.
Computed C+Q modulo 1000 directly as 800.

title: Lucas digit-box: square-moment mod $2^3$ and exponential sum mod $5^3$, coupled by CRT

identity.short_name: Lucas digit-box: square-moment mod $2^3$ and exponential sum mod $5^3$, coupled by CRT

problem_spec.summary: Use Lucas' theorem to identify indices k for which p does not divide binom(n,k) as a digit-box in base p. Compute a polynomial digit-box sum Q = sum_{k in S} k^2 modulo 2^3, and an exponential digit-box sum R = sum_{k in S} a^k modulo 5^3 using prime-power binomial expansions; then couple the residues with CRT to obtain a unique N modulo 1000.
givens: $p$ is a prime
$p\equiv 1\pmod{8}$
$a\equiv 1\pmod{5}$
For $0\le k\le n$, $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$
$S=\{k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}: p\nmid A_k\}$
$Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ and $R=\sum_{k\in S}a^k$
$N$ satisfies $N\equiv Q\pmod{8}$ and $N\equiv R\pmod{125}$
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ satisfying $N\equiv Q\pmod{8}$ and $N\equiv R\pmod{125}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: Lucas modulus and base; also makes $p^{i}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$.

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: base for the exponential sum modulo $5^3$ enabling binomial truncation.

parameter(s).name: d_0
parameter(s).purpose: least significant base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_1
parameter(s).purpose: second base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_2
parameter(s).purpose: third base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_3
parameter(s).purpose: fourth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_4
parameter(s).purpose: fifth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_5
parameter(s).purpose: sixth base-$p$ digit of $n$

invariant(s).name: fixed_six_digit_base_p_n
invariant(s).statement: $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ has base-$p$ digits $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$.

invariant(s).name: Lucas_digit_box_for_S
invariant(s).statement: Lucas' theorem implies $S=\{k: p\nmid\binom{n}{k}\}$ is the digit-box $0\le k_i\le d_i$ in base $p$.

invariant(s).name: square_moment_digit_factorization
invariant(s).statement: Expanding $k^2$ in base-$p$ digits and factoring digit moments yields a closed formula for $Q$.

invariant(s).name: mod8_collapse_when_p_is_1_mod_8
invariant(s).statement: If $p\equiv 1\pmod{8}$ then all powers $p^{i}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$, simplifying $Q\bmod 8$.

invariant(s).name: exponential_sum_digit_factorization
invariant(s).statement: $R=\sum_{k\in S}a^k$ factors as a product of digit-wise geometric sums $\sum_{d=0}^{d_i}(a^{p^i})^d$.

invariant(s).name: prime_power_binomial_truncation_mod_125
invariant(s).statement: If $a\equiv 1\pmod{5}$ then writing $a=1+5t$ and truncating the binomial expansion computes powers modulo $125$.

invariant(s).name: CRT_coupling_for_unique_N
invariant(s).statement: Since $\gcd(8,125)=1$, the pair $(Q\bmod 8,\;R\bmod 125)$ determines a unique class $N\bmod 1000$.

essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime (Lucas applies).
$p\equiv 1\pmod{8}$.
$a\equiv 1\pmod{5}$.
Normalization: output the unique $N$ with $0\le N<1000$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem uniquely determines $S$ from the digits of $n$.
Digit-box moment and exponential factorization uniquely determine $Q\bmod 8$ and $R\bmod 125$.
CRT uniquely determines $N\bmod 1000$ since $\gcd(8,125)=1$, and $0\le N<1000$ fixes $N$.

Digit-box exponential factorization + binomial truncation for $R\bmod 125$.
CRT coupling to get $N\bmod 1000$.

title: Gaussian norm representation drives a quadratic congruence modulo $q^2$ with a prime-square obstruction

identity.short_name: Gaussian norm representation drives a quadratic congruence modulo $q^2$ with a prime-square obstruction

problem_spec.summary: A prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ is encoded via its normalized Gaussian norm representation $p=x^2+y^2$, producing a canonical residue $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$ with $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$. This $t$ defines a quadratic polynomial $f(n)$ whose roots are counted modulo $q^2$. Under the guard $q\mid (t^2-4(t+\delta))$, completion of the square reduces the problem to square roots of the discriminant $D$ modulo $q^2$, and a valuation obstruction shows $D$ is not a square when $v_q(D)=1$. The output is a fixed linear form in $x,y,M,\delta$ reduced modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$.
Let $(x,y)$ be the unique integers with $x>y>0$ and $x^2+y^2=p$.
Let $\delta$ be a fixed integer parameter.
Define $t$ as the least positive residue of $x y^{-1}\pmod p$.
Define $f(n)\equiv n^2+t n+(t+\delta)\pmod{q^2}$.
Let $M=|\{0\le n\le q^2-1: f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}\}|$.
Assume the singular discriminant guard $q\mid (t^2-4(t+\delta))$.
ask: Compute the unique $A$ with $0\le A<1000$ such that $A\equiv 2x+7y+9M+\delta\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: ensures existence and normalized uniqueness of $p=x^2+y^2$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: prime-square modulus $q^2$ where square-root lifting can fail in the singular regime

parameter(s).name: delta
parameter(s).purpose: shifts the discriminant $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ and controls the $q$-adic valuation regime

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: removes unit and conjugation ambiguity so $(x,y)$ and $t$ are unique

invariant(s).name: unique_gaussian_norm_representation
invariant(s).statement: For prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ there exist integers $x,y$ with $p=x^2+y^2$, and the rule $x>y>0$ selects a unique pair.

invariant(s).name: t_is_sqrt_minus_one_mod_p
invariant(s).statement: With $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$, one has $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$.

invariant(s).name: completion_of_square_mod_q2
invariant(s).statement: For $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ and odd $q$, $f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}$ is equivalent to $(2n+t)^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$.

invariant(s).name: prime_square_sqrt_obstruction
invariant(s).statement: If $q\mid D$ but $q^2\nmid D$, then $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$ has no solutions.

essential_assumption(s): The condition $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ ensures $p=x^2+y^2$ and supports defining $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$.
The rule $x>y>0$ is essential to make $(x,y)$ and hence $t$ unique.
$q$ is odd so $2$ and $4$ are invertible modulo $q^2$, enabling completion of the square.
The guard $q\mid (t^2-4(t+\delta))$ forces the singular discriminant regime modulo $q$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Normalization $x>y>0$ gives a unique $(x,y)$ and hence a unique $t$ and $D$.
If $q\mid D$ and $v_q(D)=1$ then $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$ has no solutions, so $M=0$ is forced.
The congruence modulo $1000$ together with $0\le A<1000$ makes $A$ unique.

technique(s).name: unique_factorization_Zi_optional
technique(s).purpose: justify existence and normalized uniqueness of $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: gaussian_euclidean_algorithm
inputs: $p$ and a square root $u$ of $-1\pmod p$
technique(s).purpose: compute a Gaussian gcd to obtain an explicit representation $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: norm_to_modular_interface
technique(s).purpose: produce $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$ and deduce $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$

technique(s).name: completion_square_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: rewrite $f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}$ as $(2n+t)^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$
outputs: reduction to a square-root congruence for $D$ modulo $q^2$

technique(s).name: valuation_obstruction_prime_square
technique(s).purpose: decide solvability of $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$ in the singular case $q\mid D$
outputs: either no solutions when $v_q(D)=1$, or $q$ solutions when $v_q(D)\ge 2$

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_output
technique(s).purpose: apply the required normalization modulo $1000$

theorem(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_theorem
theorem(s).role: ensures existence and uniqueness up to signs and swap of $p=x^2+y^2$ for prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$

theorem(s).name: Euler_criterion_for_-1
theorem(s).role: implies existence of $u$ with $u^2\equiv -1\pmod p$ when $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$

theorem(s).name: Hensel_lemma_singular_obstruction
theorem(s).role: explains why a root modulo $q$ at $z\equiv 0$ does not lift to a root modulo $q^2$ unless $q^2\mid D$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local information at a prime power controls global root structure for polynomial congruences.

critical_trick(s): Compute $p=x^2+y^2$ via a Gaussian gcd using an explicit $u$ with $u^2\equiv -1\pmod p$.
Complete the square to reduce $f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}$ to $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$.
Use the valuation obstruction: $v_q(D)=1$ implies $D$ is not a square modulo $q^2$, forcing $M=0$.

compute $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$, check $q\mid D$ and $q^2\nmid D$, confirm $\sqrt{D}$ modulo $q^2$ does not exist,
and compute the final $A\bmod 1000$.

verification_notes: Cornacchia descent gives (x,y)=(902,433) and t=27746.
The discriminant has v_38711(D)=1, so M=0 modulo q^2 and the normalized answer is 837.

title: Gaussian norm representation with CRT-forced discriminant and mixed prime-power square-root counts

identity.short_name: Gaussian norm representation with CRT-forced discriminant and mixed prime-power square-root counts

problem_spec.summary: A prime p\equiv1 (mod 4) is encoded via the normalized Gaussian norm representation p=x^2+y^2 (x>y>0), producing a canonical residue t\equivx y^{-1} (mod p) with t^2\equiv-1 (mod p). A discriminant D(delta)=t^2-4(t+delta) is forced to satisfy two coupled congruence conditions modulo q^3 and r, which determine delta uniquely by CRT. Completion of the square converts roots of n^2+tn+(t+delta) modulo q^3 r^2 to square roots of D modulo q^3 r^2. The q-adic regime is singular with v_q(D)=2, while the r-adic regime is non-singular and handled by Hensel lifting. The final answer is a fixed linear form in x,y,delta and the root count, reduced modulo 999.
givens: Let p,q,r be distinct odd primes with p\equiv1 (mod 4).
Let (x,y) be the unique integers with x>y>0 and x^2+y^2=p.
Let t be the least positive residue of x y^{-1} (mod p).
Let delta be the unique integer with 0\leqdelta<q^3 r such that t^2-4(t+delta)\equivq^2 (mod q^3) and t^2-4(t+delta)\equiv2 (mod r).
Let N=q^3 r^2 and define f(n)\equivn^2+t n+(t+delta) (mod N).
Let M be the number of n (0\leqn<N) with f(n)\equiv0 (mod N).
ask: Compute the unique integer A with 0\leqA<999 such that A\equiv5x+7y+11delta+13M (mod 999).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: enables a unique normalized representation p=x^2+y^2 in Z[i]

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: sets the singular square-root regime through the constraint D\equivq^2 (mod q^3)

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from p,q with r\equiv±1 (mod 8)
parameter(s).purpose: ensures 2 is a quadratic residue mod r, enabling Hensel lifting to r^2

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: selects a unique ordered pair (x,y) and hence a unique t

invariant(s).name: unique_gaussian_norm_representation
invariant(s).statement: For prime p\equiv1 (mod 4) there exist integers x,y with p=x^2+y^2, and the rule x>y>0 selects a unique pair.

invariant(s).name: t_is_sqrt_minus_one_mod_p
invariant(s).statement: With t\equivx y^{-1} (mod p), one has t^2\equiv-1 (mod p).

invariant(s).name: crt_forces_delta
invariant(s).statement: Because D(delta)=t^2-4(t+delta) is affine in delta and gcd(q^3,r)=1, the congruences D\equivq^2 (mod q^3) and D\equiv2 (mod r) determine a unique delta (mod q^3 r), hence a unique 0\leqdelta<q^3 r.

invariant(s).name: forced_q_adic_regime_v_equals_2
invariant(s).statement: From D\equivq^2 (mod q^3) one has v_q(D)=2 and D/q^2\equiv1 (mod q).

invariant(s).name: forced_r_adic_regime_unit_residue
invariant(s).statement: From D\equiv2 (mod r) with r\neq2, one has r∤D and (D/r)=(2/r)=1 when r\equiv±1 (mod 8).

invariant(s).name: completion_of_square_bijection_mod_N
invariant(s).statement: For the odd modulus N=q^3 r^2 and D=t^2-4(t+delta), f(n)\equiv0 (mod N) is equivalent to (2n+t)^2\equivD (mod N), and n↦2n+t is a bijection on Z/NZ.

invariant(s).name: square_root_count_mod_q3
invariant(s).statement: Under v_q(D)=2 and D/q^2\equiv1 (mod q), the congruence z^2\equivD (mod q^3) has exactly 2q solutions.

invariant(s).name: square_root_count_mod_r2
invariant(s).statement: Under r∤D and (D/r)=1, the congruence z^2\equivD (mod r^2) has exactly 2 solutions.

invariant(s).name: crt_root_count_multiplicativity
invariant(s).statement: Since gcd(q^3,r^2)=1, the number of solutions to z^2\equivD (mod q^3 r^2) equals the product of the numbers of solutions modulo q^3 and r^2.

essential_assumption(s): p\equiv1 (mod 4) so that p=x^2+y^2 and t\equivx y^{-1} (mod p) is defined.
The rule x>y>0 is essential to make (x,y) and hence t unique.
q and r are odd and distinct, so gcd(2,q^3 r^2)=1 and CRT applies between q^3 and r^2.
r\equiv±1 (mod 8) ensures (2/r)=1, enabling the r^2 square-root count via Hensel lifting.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Normalization x>y>0 gives a unique (x,y) and hence a unique t and affine discriminant D(delta).
The coupled congruences on D(delta) modulo q^3 and r determine a unique delta (mod q^3 r) by CRT; the range 0\leqdelta<q^3 r selects a unique integer.
Given D, the number M of roots of f(n) modulo q^3 r^2 is forced by completion of the square, prime-power square-root counts (singular at q^3, Hensel at r^2), and CRT multiplicativity.
The congruence modulo 999 together with 0\leqA<999 makes A unique.

technique(s).name: completion_square_prime_power

outputs: reduction of root counting to z^2\equivD (mod N)

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_output

theorem(s).role: existence and uniqueness up to signs and swap of p=x^2+y^2 for prime p\equiv1 (mod 4)

theorem(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm_in_Zi
theorem(s).role: enables computation of a Gaussian gcd to extract the sum-of-two-squares representation

theorem(s).role: uniquely solves coupled congruences modulo coprime moduli, used for delta and for combining root sets

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Coupled local congruence constraints determine global arithmetic data via CRT.

critical_trick(s): Extract (x,y) from p using a Gaussian gcd, then compute t\equivx y^{-1} (mod p).
Force delta by coupled congruences on D(delta) and solve via CRT.
Complete the square to reduce f(n)\equiv0 to z^2\equivD, count square roots modulo q^3 (singular) and r^2 (Hensel), then recombine by CRT.

title: CRT-count of units with forced local orders, Legendre filtering, and order lifting to a prime square

identity.short_name: CRT-count of units with forced local orders, Legendre filtering, and order lifting to a prime square

problem_spec.summary:
Count residue classes x modulo a product of prime powers using CRT, where local congruence conditions force
specific local multiplicative orders and a global order constraint forces an order-lift on a prime-square component.
A Legendre-symbol constraint is converted to an exponent constraint by Euler's criterion, and the remaining count is
obtained by exact-order counting inside a cyclic unit group modulo an odd prime square (verified by an explicit generator).
givens: M=2^6 \cdot 3^4 \cdot 7^2 \cdot 19^2.
x is a residue class modulo M with gcd(x,M)=1.

Constraints: ord_M(x)=2736; x\equiv3 (mod 8); x\equiv1 (mod 27) but x
eq1 (mod 81); x^3\equiv1 (mod 49) but x
eq1 (mod 7); (x/19)=1 and x^3
eq1 (mod 19).
ask: Let C be the number of such residue classes x (mod M). Output the unique A in {0,...,999} with A\equivC (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: fixed_instance
parameter(s).purpose: This reviewed Gen-4 artifact is a single concrete instance of the daughter family.

invariant(s).name: crt_decomposition
invariant(s).statement:
Since 64,81,49,361 are pairwise coprime and M=64 \cdot 81 \cdot 49 \cdot 361, CRT gives a bijection between x (mod M) and its tuple of residues
(x (mod 64), x (mod 81), x (mod 49), x (mod 361)).

invariant(s).name: lcm_of_local_orders
invariant(s).statement: For gcd(x,M)=1, ord_M(x)=lcm(ord_64(x), ord_81(x), ord_49(x), ord_361(x)).

invariant(s).name: forced_orders_from_local_constraints
invariant(s).statement:
The constraints x\equiv3 (mod 8), x\equiv1 (mod 27) but not (mod 81), and x^3\equiv1 (mod 49) but x\neq1 (mod 7) force
ord_64(x)=16, ord_81(x)=3, ord_49(x)=3.

invariant(s).name: legendre_to_order9_mod19
invariant(s).statement: Euler criterion turns (x/19)=1 into x^9\equiv1 (mod 19); together with x^3\neq1 (mod 19) this forces ord_19(x)=9.

invariant(s).name: global_order_forces_prime_square_lift
invariant(s).statement:
Because ord_M(x)=2^4 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 19 and the other local orders are not divisible by 19, the 19-factor must come from ord_361(x),
forcing 19 | ord_361(x). Combined with ord_19(x)=9, a kernel-exponent argument forces ord_361(x)=171.

invariant(s).name: cyclic_units_mod_19_square
invariant(s).statement: The unit group modulo 19^2 has order φ(19^2)=342 and is cyclic; in fact 2 has order 342 modulo 361, so it generates the group.

essential_assumption(s): CRT applies because the prime-power factors are pairwise coprime.
Legendre symbol is evaluated only for gcd(x,19)=1, guaranteed by gcd(x,M)=1.
uniqueness_certificate(s):
Local constraints force fixed local orders and fixed local counts at 64,81,49.
The Legendre constraint forces ord_19(x)=9, and the global order constraint forces 19|ord_361(x).
Under ord_19=9, the only possible values of ord_361 are 9 or 171; the extra divisibility by 19 forces ord_361=171 uniquely.
Exact-order counting in the cyclic group (Z/361Z)^× gives exactly φ(171)=108 such residues, and CRT gives a bijection between global
solutions and the product of the local solution sets, forcing a unique total count C and hence a unique A\equivC (mod 1000).

technique(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
technique(s).purpose: Split the global constraints into local constraints and combine local choices bijectively.
outputs: Bijection between S and a Cartesian product of local solution sets

technique(s).name: order_lcm_in_direct_product
inputs: CRT group isomorphism on unit groups
technique(s).purpose: Translate the global order constraint into an lcm constraint on local orders.
outputs: Forces divisibility constraints on the remaining component order

technique(s).name: binomial_expansion_mod_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: Show (1+27t)^3\equiv1 (mod 81) and force ord_81=3.

technique(s).name: cyclic_group_root_count
technique(s).purpose: Count cube roots of unity modulo 7.

technique(s).name: hensel_lifting
technique(s).purpose: Lift each root uniquely to modulo 49 and preserve nontriviality.
outputs: Exactly 3 roots modulo 49; 2 after excluding the 1 mod 7 root

technique(s).name: two_adic_exponent_computation
technique(s).purpose: Compute u^8 modulo 64 uniformly and force ord_64(u)=16.

technique(s).name: euler_criterion_legendre_filter
technique(s).purpose: Convert a quadratic-residue condition to x^9\equiv1 (mod 19).

technique(s).name: kernel_exponent_order_lifting
inputs: Reduction map (Z/361Z)^× -> (Z/19Z)^× with kernel of size 19
technique(s).purpose: Show that under ord_19=9, the only possible ord_361 are 9 or 171, and use the global constraint to force 171.

technique(s).name: exact_order_counting
technique(s).purpose: Count elements of exact order 171.

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: Bijection of residues and decomposition of unit groups modulo coprime factors.

theorem(s).name: hensel_lemma_simple_root
theorem(s).role: Unique lifting of simple roots from modulo 7 to modulo 49.

theorem(s).name: euler_criterion
theorem(s).role: Characterizes the Legendre symbol by an exponent test modulo an odd prime.

theorem(s).name: order_divisibility_under_homomorphism
theorem(s).role: The order of an element maps to a divisor under a group homomorphism; used for mod 19 vs mod 361.

theorem(s).name: totient_exact_order_count
theorem(s).role: In a cyclic group, the number of elements of order d is φ(d).

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local-to-global structure: CRT plus lcm coupling of multiplicative orders.

critical_trick(s): Force fixed local orders on 2-, 3-, and 7-power components, then use the global order to force an order lift at 19^2.
Convert (x/19)=1 to x^9\equiv1 (mod 19) via Euler criterion, then exclude x^3\equiv1 to get order 9.
Count exact order 171 elements in (Z/361Z)^× using cyclicity shown by an explicit generator.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: gcd computations and modular arithmetic.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: Enumerate the Cartesian product of local solution sets (size 3456).

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Compute multiplicative orders, Legendre symbols, and CRT combinations for independent verification.

task(s).name: local_set_enumeration
task(s).reason: Enumerate S_64, S_81, S_49, and S_361 and confirm their sizes and local orders.

task(s).name: crt_product_verification
task(s).reason: Combine all local choices via CRT (3456 combinations) and verify all defining conditions including ord_M(x)=2736.

verification_notes: Enumerated the admissible local sets modulo 64, 81, 49, and 361 with sizes 8, 2, 2, and 108.
CRT multiplication gives C=3456, so the normalized count is 456.

title: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots)

identity.short_name: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots)

problem_spec.summary: Given integers $a,n$ with $\gcd(a,n)=1$, determine the multiplicative order $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ (the least $t\ge 1$ with $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$), and use it to evaluate exponential congruences.
givens: $n\in\mathbb{N},\ n\ge 2$
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$
ask: Find the least positive integer $t$ such that $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: varies the modulus; determines the unit group $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: selects an element of the unit group whose cycle length is sought

parameter(s).name: factorization_hint_mode
parameter(s).purpose: controls whether the instance supplies factorization information to compute the order efficiently

invariant(s).name: Euler_cycle_bound
invariant(s).statement: By Euler, $a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n$, so $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ exists and divides $\varphi(n)$.

invariant(s).name: order_divides_phi
invariant(s).statement: $t=\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ is the minimal positive period of the sequence $a^k\bmod n$, hence $t\mid\varphi(n)$.

essential_assumption(s): Coprimality $\gcd(a,n)=1$ (unit condition) is required so that a multiplicative order is defined.
If $n$ is not prime and no factorization data are given, computing $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ may be hard; the family allows a "hint mode" parameter to keep instances decidable.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The set $\{k\ge 1: a^k\equiv 1\pmod n\}$ is nonempty (Euler) and closed under gcd; its least element exists and is unique by well-ordering, hence $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ is well-defined and unique.

technique(s).name: Euler_theorem
technique(s).purpose: guarantee existence of a finite exponent cycle
outputs: certificate that some exponent (namely $\varphi(n)$) returns $1$ modulo $n$

technique(s).name: Fermat_little
technique(s).purpose: specialize Euler when $n$ is prime; simplifies order computation

technique(s).name: order_mod_n
technique(s).purpose: define and manipulate the minimal exponent period

technique(s).name: exponent_cycles
technique(s).purpose: reduce large exponents using periodicity

technique(s).name: lifting_solutions_mod_p_to_p^k_optional
inputs: solutions mod $p$ and local derivative condition
technique(s).purpose: (optional) lift order/congruence information from $p$ to $p^k$ when $n$ has prime power factors
outputs: constraints on order modulo prime powers

theorem(s).name: Euler_phi
theorem(s).role: provides the size of the unit group modulo $n$ and an exponent bound

theorem(s).name: Wilson_optional
theorem(s).role: optional tool to compute factorial residues modulo primes and deduce order information in special constructions

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Units modulo $n$ form a finite multiplicative group; every element has finite order.

critical_trick(s): Use $a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n$ to restrict the order to divisors of $\varphi(n)$, then test divisors (often via prime factorization of $\varphi(n)$).
Exploit prime-power structure (optional) to control orders when $n$ is not prime.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: factorial for $a=30!-3$, gcd/lcm arithmetic, and built-in pow(a,e,n) for modular exponentiation.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: totient and factorint to compute and factor $\varphi(n)$ for divisor-reduction order computation and cross-checking.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Verified gcd(a,n)=1 and that the computed t satisfies pow(a,t,n)=1.
- "Computed t by divisor-reduction from phi(n): start t=phi(n) and repeatedly divide by prime factors q of t when pow(a,t/q,n)=1."
- Cross-checked component orders modulo 31, 31^2, and 5 and verified lcm(310,4)=620 gives pow(a,620,n)=1.

NT5 preflight attempts (cheap strategies that did not reveal t=620):
- "Small-mod-only attack on mods {2,4,5,7,8,11}: lcm of the resulting small orders was 60, not 620 (mods 3 and 9 were skipped since gcd(a,m) != 1)."
- "Tiny bounded scan: searched for the least t with pow(a,t,n)=1 in the range 1 <= t <= 500; none found."
- "Simple template probes: tested exponents {phi(n), phi(n)/2, phi(n)/3, phi(n)/4, phi(n)/5, 30, 930}; the smallest among these giving 1 was 1240, not the true order 620."

NT6 collision resistance:
- Plausible wrong-branch guess t=310 (ignoring the mod 5 component) differs from the true t=620 (mod 1000).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independent brute-force order computation by incremental multiplication until the first return to 1 modulo n, confirming t=620.

verification_notes: Reduced a=30!-3 modulo n=4805 and computed ord_n(a) directly as 620.
The order was recovered by divisor search inside phi(n)=3720.

title: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary:
Let $\operatorname{ord}_m(a)$ denote the multiplicative order of $a$ modulo $m$ (for $\gcd(a,m)=1$), and for a prime $\ell$ let $v_\ell(x)$ be the exponent of $\ell$ in the prime factorization of the integer $x$. Fix odd primes $p
eq q$ and an integer $k\ge 2$, and suppose $r=2q+1$ is also prime with $r
eq p$. Put $n=p^k r$ and choose an integer $a$ coprime to $n$ such that $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$, $v_p(a^q-1)=1$, and $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ but $a
ot\equiv -1\pmod r$; additionally assume $v_q(a-1)=1$. Consider the residue sequence $u_m\equiv a^m\pmod n$ and let $t$ be its least positive period.
givens: $p,q$ are odd primes with $p\neq q$ and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 2}$.
$r=2q+1$ is prime and $r\neq p$; set $n=p^k r$.
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$ satisfies $\gcd(a,n)=1$.
$\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$ and $v_p(a^q-1)=1$.
$a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ and $a\not\equiv -1\pmod r$.
For $m\ge 0$, define $u_m\equiv a^m\pmod n$.
ask: Stage 1 (order/period): Let $t$ be the least positive integer such that $u_{m+t}=u_m$ for all $m\ge 0$ (equivalently, $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$). Determine $t$ explicitly in terms of $p,q,k$.
Stage 2 (valuation output): Compute $N\equiv t\,(v_p(a^t-1)+v_q(a^t-1))\pmod{1000}$ and report the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power factor $p^k$ that forces nontrivial order lifting

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: forces a prescribed nontrivial prime divisor of $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: controls the depth of the prime-power modulus and the size of the lifted order/valuation

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).domain: integer $a$ with $\gcd(a,p)=\gcd(a,r)=1$, $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$, $v_p(a^q-1)=1$, $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ with $a\not\equiv -1\pmod r$, and $v_q(a-1)=1$, where $r=2q+1$ is prime and $n=p^k r$.
parameter(s).purpose: a unit whose order is lifted from $p$ to $p^k$ using valuation data, while simultaneously forcing a complementary order condition modulo $r$

invariant(s).name: Euler_cycle_bound
invariant(s).statement: By Euler, $a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n$, so $\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ exists and divides $\varphi(n)$.

invariant(s).name: order_divides_phi
invariant(s).statement: $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ is the minimal positive period of the sequence $a^m\bmod n$, hence $t\mid\varphi(n)$.

invariant(s).name: safe_prime_component
invariant(s).statement: $q$ is an odd prime and $r=2q+1$ is prime, so $(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$ is cyclic of order $2q$.

invariant(s).name: order_mod_r_forced
invariant(s).statement: From $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ and $a\not\equiv -1\pmod r$ (with $q$ odd), it follows that $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$.

invariant(s).name: p_power_lift_via_valuation
invariant(s).statement: The conditions $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$ and $v_p(a^q-1)=1$ force $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=q\,p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: forced_divisor_and_nondivisor_in_global_period
invariant(s).statement: With $n=p^k r$ and $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$, we have $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a),\operatorname{ord}_r(a))$, hence $q\,p^{k-1}\mid t$ and $2\mid t$, but $p^k\nmid t$ and $4\nmid t$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_nontriviality_two_primes
invariant(s).statement: The guards $v_p(a^q-1)=1$ and $v_q(a-1)=1$ ensure $v_p(a^t-1)=k\ge 2$ and $v_q(a^t-1)=2$ by LTE, so Stage 2 cannot collapse to a valuation-0 edge case.

essential_assumption(s): Coprimality $\gcd(a,n)=1$ (unit condition) is required so that a multiplicative order is defined.
The valuation guards $v_p(a^q-1)=1$ and $v_q(a-1)=1$ are essential; without them the lifted order modulo $p^k$ and the valuation term in Stage 2 are not forced.
Instances are intended to be solved by decomposing the order locally and then applying LTE-based lifting; brute-force testing of divisors of $\varphi(n)$ is excluded by design for this family.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The constraints imply $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=q p^{k-1}$ (order lifting with $v_p(a^q-1)=1$) and $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$ (safe-prime modulus with $a^q\equiv -1$ and $a\not\equiv -1$). Since $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$, the order modulo $n=p^k r$ is uniquely

verification_notes: Order lifting from 89 to 89^4 gives ord=7754659, while a ≡ -2 mod 23 gives ord_23(a)=22.
Thus t=15509318; LTE then gives v_89(a^t-1)=4 and v_11(a^t-1)=2.

title: Multiplicative order via principal units + quadratic reciprocity (halved-period valuation mix)

identity.short_name: Multiplicative order via principal units + quadratic reciprocity (halved-period valuation mix)

problem_spec.summary: Modulus n=p^k r with p an odd prime and r=2q+1 a safe prime satisfying r\equiv3 (mod 8). A unit a is specified simultaneously as a principal unit modulo p^k (a\equiv1+p) and as a fixed residue modulo r (a\equiv2), so the order modulo p^k is forced by a principal-unit/LTE argument while the order modulo r is forced by quadratic reciprocity plus Euler's criterion. An extra congruence a\equiv1+q (mod q^2) forces a controlled q-adic valuation. The task is to compute the global period t=ord_n(a) and then a normalized quantity involving t/2 and two valuations.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are odd primes with $p\ne q$, and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 2}$.
$q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $r=2q+1$ is prime with $r\ne p$; set $n=p^k r$.
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$ satisfies the congruences $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$, $a\equiv 1+q\pmod{q^2}$, and $a\equiv 2\pmod r$.
For $m\ge 0$, define $u_m\equiv a^m\pmod n$ and let $t$ be the least positive period (equivalently $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$).
ask: Stage 1 (period as order): Determine $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ explicitly in terms of $p,q,k$ under the above constraints.
Stage 2 (normalized valuation mix): Let $V=\frac{t}{2}\bigl(v_p(a^t-1)+v_q(a^t-1)\bigr)$ (it is forced that $t$ is even). Compute the unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N\equiv V\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power modulus $p^k$ whose order is forced by principal-unit structure

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime $q\ge 5$ with $q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $r=2q+1$ prime, $r\ne p$
parameter(s).purpose: produces a safe prime $r$ with $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$ so $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$ and hence $\operatorname{ord}_r(2)=2q$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: controls the depth of the principal-unit order and the forced $p$-adic valuation

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).domain: integer a satisfying a\equiv1+p (mod p^k), a\equiv1+q (mod q^2), and a\equiv2 (mod r), where r=2q+1 is prime and n=p^k r.
parameter(s).purpose: couples a principal-unit order computation modulo p^k with a quadratic-residue forcing modulo r and a controlled q-adic valuation

invariant(s).name: CRT_admissibility
invariant(s).statement: The moduli $p^k$, $q^2$, and $r$ are pairwise coprime, so the congruence system defining $a$ is solvable (CRT).

invariant(s).name: unit_condition
invariant(s).statement: From $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ and $a\equiv 2\pmod r$, we have $\gcd(a,n)=1$, so $\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ exists.

invariant(s).name: principal_unit_order_forced
invariant(s).statement: The constraint $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ forces $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: two_is_nonresidue_mod_r
invariant(s).statement: Since $q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $r=2q+1$, we have $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$, hence $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$.

invariant(s).name: order_mod_r_forced_by_Euler_criterion
invariant(s).statement: With $a\equiv 2\pmod r$ and $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$, Euler's criterion forces $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$.

invariant(s).name: global_period_lcm
invariant(s).statement: Since $n=p^k r$ with $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$, we have $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a),\operatorname{ord}_r(a))=\mathrm{lcm}(p^{k-1},2q)=2q\,p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: forced_evenness
invariant(s).statement: The period $t$ is even (because $2\mid \operatorname{ord}_r(a)$), so $t/2\in\mathbb{Z}$ is well-defined.

invariant(s).name: forced_two_valuations
invariant(s).statement: The guards $v_p(a-1)=1$ and $v_q(a-1)=1$ together with $t=2q\,p^{k-1}$ force $v_p(a^t-1)=k$ and $v_q(a^t-1)=2$ by LTE.

essential_assumption(s): $p,q,r$ are odd primes with $p\ne q$, $r=2q+1$ prime, and $q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ (ensures $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$ so $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$).
The principal-unit constraint $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ is essential; it forces $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$ and fixes the $p$-adic valuation via LTE.
The residue constraint $a\equiv 2\pmod r$ is essential; combined with quadratic reciprocity and Euler criterion it forces $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$.
The valuation guard $a\equiv 1+q\pmod{q^2}$ is essential; without it, $v_q(a^t-1)$ is not forced.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The congruences force $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$ (principal-unit order) and, since $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$, quadratic reciprocity gives $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$; with $a\equiv 2\pmod r$, Euler's criterion forces $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$. With $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$ this uniquely forces

verification_notes: Principal-unit forcing gives ord_37^3(a)=1369, and 2 has order 58 modulo 59.
The global period is t=79402; LTE gives v_37(a^t-1)=3 and v_29(a^t-1)=2.

title: Valuation-filtered p-th roots of unity modulo p^a q^b

identity.short_name: Valuation-filtered p-th roots of unity modulo p^a q^b

problem_spec.summary: Fix distinct odd primes p,q with p | (q-1) and integers a>=2, b>=1. Let M=p^a q^b and count residue classes x mod M satisfying x^p \equiv 1 together with mixed p-adic valuation constraints on x-1 at primes p and q. The solution splits locally via CRT, uses LTE to force the p-adic congruence under the exact valuation, uses cyclicity of (Z/q^bZ)^× plus Hensel lifting to locate the unique root congruent to 1 (mod q), then multiplies local counts.
givens: Distinct odd primes p,q with p < q.
Integers a>=2 and b>=1.
Divisibility guard p | (q-1).
Define M = p^a q^b.
Define S to be the set of residue classes x (mod M) such that:
x^p \equiv 1 (mod M),
ask: Let C = |S|. Output the unique integer A in {0,...,999} with A \equiv C (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: exponent in x^p \equiv 1 and the p-adic valuation jump via LTE

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from p with p | (q-1)
parameter(s).purpose: ensures existence of nontrivial p-th roots of unity modulo q and hence modulo q^b

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: sets the p-adic depth; makes v_p(x-1)=a-1 >= 1 so LTE forces x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a)

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: sets the q-adic lifting depth; Hensel lifting preserves root classes from mod q to mod q^b

invariant(s).name: crt_local_global_factorization
invariant(s).statement: For M=p^a q^b with gcd(p^a,q^b)=1, residues x mod M correspond bijectively to pairs (x mod p^a, x mod q^b), and the defining congruences/valuations split into local constraints.

invariant(s).name: lte_valuation_jump_at_p
invariant(s).statement: If v_p(x-1)=a-1 (>=1) then LTE gives v_p(x^p-1)=a, so x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a) is forced by the valuation constraint.

invariant(s).name: cyclic_units_at_q
invariant(s).statement: For odd prime q, (Z/q^bZ)^× is cyclic of order φ(q^b)=q^{b-1}(q-1).

invariant(s).name: hensel_simple_root_lifts_preserve_mod_q_class
invariant(s).statement: For f(X)=X^p-1 with q not dividing p, every root mod q is simple and lifts uniquely to a root mod q^b, preserving its residue class mod q.

invariant(s).name: q_valuation_filter_excludes_exactly_one_root
invariant(s).statement: When p | (q-1), X^p \equiv 1 (mod q^b) has exactly p roots, and exactly one is \equiv 1 (mod q); thus v_q(x-1)=0 keeps precisely p-1 roots.

essential_assumption(s): p and q are distinct odd primes so CRT applies and LTE/Hensel are valid in the stated forms.
The guard p | (q-1) is required so that nontrivial p-th roots of unity exist modulo q (hence modulo q^b); otherwise S may be empty.
a >= 2 ensures v_p(x-1)=a-1 >= 1 so the LTE forcing step is non-degenerate.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT splits the set S into independent local sets S_p and S_q. LTE forces the p-adic congruence once v_p(x-1)=a-1 and yields exactly p-1 classes modulo p^a. Cyclicity of (Z/q^bZ)^× gives exactly p roots to x^p \equiv 1 modulo q^b, and Hensel shows exactly one is \equiv1 (mod q); the filter v_q(x-1)=0 removes it, leaving p-1. Therefore C=(p-1)^2 is forced and the normalized output mod 1000 is unique.

technique(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: modulus factorization M=p^a q^b with gcd(p^a,q^b)=1
technique(s).purpose: split global conditions into independent local problems and multiply local counts

technique(s).name: p_adic_valuation_filtering
inputs: exact valuation constraints v_p(x-1)=a-1 and v_q(x-1)=0
technique(s).purpose: parametrize residue classes by exact divisibility/coprimality conditions and count admissible classes
outputs: local counts |S_p|=p-1 and |S_q|=p-1 under the stated guards

technique(s).name: lifting_the_exponent
technique(s).purpose: force v_p(x^p-1)=v_p(x-1)+1, turning the p-adic congruence into a consequence of the valuation constraint
outputs: x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a) is automatic once v_p(x-1)=a-1

technique(s).name: cyclic_group_root_count
technique(s).purpose: count solutions to y^p=1 via gcd(p, φ(q^b))
outputs: exactly p solutions to y^p \equiv 1 (mod q^b) when p | (q-1)

technique(s).name: hensel_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift roots from mod q to mod q^b uniquely and preserve the mod-q class
outputs: certify that exactly one p-th root is \equiv1 (mod q), enabling the valuation exclusion

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: bijection between residues mod p^a q^b and pairs mod p^a and q^b

theorem(s).name: lifting_the_exponent_lemma
theorem(s).role: gives v_p(x^p-1)=v_p(x-1)+1 when p is odd and p | (x-1)

theorem(s).name: primitive_root_theorem_for_prime_powers
theorem(s).role: ensures (Z/q^bZ)^× is cyclic for odd prime q

theorem(s).name: cyclic_group_root_count_lemma
theorem(s).role: in a cyclic group of order n, y^p=1 has exactly gcd(p,n) solutions

theorem(s).name: hensel_lemma_simple_root
theorem(s).role: lifts each simple root mod q uniquely to a root mod q^b, preserving its class mod q

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local-to-global structure via prime power moduli: CRT reduces global congruences to local ones, and p-adic valuation controls divisibility and root lifting.

critical_trick(s): Use LTE to turn v_p(x-1)=a-1 into the forced congruence x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a), collapsing the p-adic side to a pure valuation count.
Use cyclicity of (Z/q^bZ)^× to count p-th roots modulo q^b, then use Hensel lifting to show only one root is \equiv1 (mod q), so the filter v_q(x-1)=0 excludes exactly one.
Multiply local counts using CRT to obtain C=(p-1)^2 and reduce modulo 1000.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks consistent with the intended math path:
- Verified gcd(p^a,q^b)=1 and computed M=p^a q^b.
- Enumerated the p-adic candidates x_p = 1 + p^(a-1)t (t=1..p-1) and verified v_p(x_p-1)=a-1 and x_p^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a).
- Enumerated all p-th roots modulo q^b via a primitive root and verified there are exactly p of them; confirmed only one is \equiv1 (mod q), so the filter v_q(x-1)=0 leaves p-1.
Preflight attempts:
- "Attempt A: naive guess C_guess=p-1 gives 22, but computed C=484 (mismatch)."
- "Attempt B: bounded search x in [0,200000) finds 0 solutions (the smallest solution exceeds this range), so brute scanning cannot shortcut the count."
- "Attempt C: dropping condition (iii) changes the count to C_relaxed=(p-1)p=506, showing partial information leaves multiple candidates in {0,...,999}."

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent verification:
- Constructed all solutions x mod M by CRT-combining the 22 admissible x_p classes with the 22 admissible x_q classes (total 484).
- Directly checked for every constructed x that (i) x^p \equiv 1 (mod M), (ii) v_p(x-1)=a-1, (iii) v_q(x-1)=0, and confirmed all 484 are distinct modulo M.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalized the parent core congruence polynomial (x-1, up to nonzero scaling) versus the daughter core polynomial (x^p-1) using sympy expand+simplify+together+factor and monic Poly normalization.
Tried deterministic one-variable disguises: x->x, -x, 1-x, x+1, x-1, 1/x, -1/x (clearing denominators and comparing monic numerators).
No transform matched x-1; degrees differ (1 vs p), so the families are not equivalent under the allowed disguises.

final_answer.normalization: A is the unique integer in {0,...,999} with A \equiv C (mod 1000).

verification_notes: The p-adic side contributes 22 classes with v_23(x-1)=9.
Enumerating x^23=1 modulo 47 gives 23 simple roots; all but the root 1 lift to admissible classes modulo 47^8, so the q-adic side contributes 22.

title: CRT-forced global order with a prime-square lift and binomial valuation coupling (mod 999)

identity.short_name: CRT-forced global order with a prime-square lift and binomial valuation coupling (mod 999)

problem_spec.summary: An integer a is defined by CRT constraints modulo a mixed modulus 2^m p^k q^4 r^2, where p and q are odd primes and r=2q+1 is a safe prime. Local orders at p^k and q^4 are forced by odd-prime LTE on principal-unit congruences; the 2^m component is forced via 2-adic LTE. The safe-prime component is strengthened to r^2 using an order-lifting lemma (a nontrivial check modulo r^2 for the chosen residue class). The global period t=ord_n(a) is the lcm of local orders. A second stage couples t to binomial valuations v_p(C(2t,t)) and v_2(C(2t,t)) using Kummer (carries) and Legendre (digit-sum) formulas, and the final output is W reduced modulo 999.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are odd primes with $p\ne q$.
$r=2q+1$ is prime and $r\ne p$.
Integers $k\ge 3$, $m\ge 6$, and $h$ with $1\le h<k$ are fixed.
$a\in\mathbb Z$ satisfies $a\equiv 1+p^h\pmod{p^k}$, $a\equiv 1+q^2\pmod{q^4}$, $a\equiv 3\pmod{2^m}$, and $a\equiv 2\pmod{r^2}$.
For $j\ge 0$, $u_j\equiv a^j\pmod n$, and $t$ is the least positive integer with $u_{j+t}=u_j$ for all $j\ge 0$.
ask: Determine $N\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ such that $N\equiv W\pmod{999}$, where $W=\frac{t}{16}(v_2(a^t-1)+v_p(a^t-1))+v_2\binom{2t}{t}+v_p\binom{2t}{t}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime-power modulus $p^k$ with principal-unit depth parameter $h$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the $q^4$ modulus and the safe prime $r$

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: safe-prime modulus used at prime-square level $r^2$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the principal-unit congruence modulo $p^k$

parameter(s).name: h
parameter(s).purpose: forces $v_p(a-1)=h$ and the local order $p^{k-h}$

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: introduces a $2^m$ component with forced order divisible by $16$

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).domain: integer satisfying the stated CRT congruences modulo $2^m$, $p^k$, $q^4$, and $r^2$
parameter(s).purpose: simultaneously forces local orders and enables binomial-valuation coupling via the global order $t$

invariant(s).name: CRT_solvability
invariant(s).statement: The moduli $2^m$, $p^k$, $q^4$, and $r^2$ are pairwise coprime, so the defining system for $a$ is solvable by CRT.

invariant(s).name: unit_condition
invariant(s).statement: The congruences force $\gcd(a,n)=1$, so $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ is defined.

invariant(s).name: forced_principal_unit_orders
invariant(s).statement: Odd-prime LTE implies $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-h}$ and $\operatorname{ord}_{q^4}(a)=q^2$ from $v_p(a-1)=h$ and $v_q(a-1)=2$.

invariant(s).name: forced_2power_order_from_residue_3
invariant(s).statement: From $a\equiv 3\pmod{2^m}$ with $m\ge 3$, 2-adic LTE forces $\operatorname{ord}_{2^m}(a)=2^{m-2}$, hence $16\mid t$ when $m\ge 6$.

invariant(s).name: safe_prime_quadratic_character_of_2
invariant(s).statement: If $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$, then $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)=-1$, so Euler's criterion forces $2^{(r-1)/2}\equiv -1\pmod r$ and $\operatorname{ord}_r(2)=r-1$.

invariant(s).name: prime_square_order_lift
invariant(s).statement: If additionally $2^{r-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$, then an order-lifting lemma forces $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(2)=r(r-1)$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(a)=r(r-1)$ when $a\equiv 2\pmod{r^2}$.

invariant(s).name: global_period_is_lcm
invariant(s).statement: With pairwise coprime factors, $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ equals the least common multiple of the local orders.

invariant(s).name: binomial_valuation_coupling
invariant(s).statement: Kummer and Legendre formulas express $v_p\binom{2t}{t}$ and $v_2\binom{2t}{t}$ in terms of base-$p$ carries and base-2 digit sums of $t$, making these valuations deterministic functions of the forced global order.

essential_assumption(s): $m\ge 6$ so that $\operatorname{ord}_{2^m}(a)=2^{m-2}$ implies $16\mid t$ and $\frac{t}{16}$ is integral.
The safe prime $r=2q+1$ satisfies the non-Wieferich-type condition $2^{r-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$ so the order-lifting lemma determines $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(2)$.
$1\le h<k$ so $v_p(a-1)=h$ is fixed and the $p^k$ order is nontrivial.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The CRT congruences fix the residue class of a modulo n. Odd-prime LTE forces the local principal-unit orders at p^k and q^4. 2-adic LTE forces the local order at 2^m and ensures 16 divides the global period t. Quadratic reciprocity and Euler's criterion force the order modulo r, and the order-lifting lemma (with the stated non-Wieferich guard) forces the strengthened order modulo r^2. Hence t is uniquely forced as the lcm of these local orders. With this t, LTE fixes v_2(a^t-1) and v_p(a^t-1). Finally, Kummer (carries) and Legendre (digit-sum) formulas fix v_p(C(2t,t)) and v_2(C(2t,t)) uniquely from t. Therefore W and its reduction modulo 999 yield a unique N in {0,...,998}.

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: pairwise coprime moduli $2^m$, $p^k$, $q^4$, $r^2$ and specified residues
technique(s).purpose: guarantee existence of $a$ and allow reduction to local computations

technique(s).name: order_mod_n
technique(s).purpose: reduce $\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ to the lcm of local orders

technique(s).name: LTE_v_p_valuation
technique(s).purpose: compute valuations $v_p(a^n-1)$ and force principal-unit orders modulo prime powers
outputs: explicit local orders at $p^k$ and $q^4$ and valuations at exponent $t$

technique(s).name: LTE_v2_valuation
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_2(a^t-1)$ and force the $2^m$ local order

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: evaluate $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)$
outputs: quadratic character information used with Euler's criterion

technique(s).name: Euler_criterion
technique(s).purpose: force $a^{(r-1)/2}\equiv -1\pmod r$ when $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)=-1$

technique(s).name: order_lifting_prime_square
inputs: order modulo $r$ and a nontriviality check modulo $r^2$
technique(s).purpose: lift $\operatorname{ord}_r$ to $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}$

technique(s).name: Kummer_carries
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p\binom{2t}{t}$ via carry counting

technique(s).name: Legendre_factorial_valuation
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_2\binom{2t}{t}$ via $v_2(n!)=n-s_2(n)$

technique(s).name: modular_reduction
technique(s).purpose: produce the unique normalized output $N\in\{0,\dots,998\}$

theorem(s).name: Chinese_Remainder_Theorem
theorem(s).role: existence of $a$ satisfying simultaneous congruences

theorem(s).name: Order_mod_coprime_product_lcm
theorem(s).role: $\operatorname{ord}_{M_1M_2}(a)=\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{M_1}(a),\operatorname{ord}_{M_2}(a))$ for coprime moduli

theorem(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_Lemma_LTE_odd_prime_case
theorem(s).role: $v_\ell(x^n-1)=v_\ell(x-1)+v_\ell(n)$ for odd $\ell\mid(x-1)$

theorem(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_Lemma_LTE_2_adic_case
theorem(s).role: $v_2(x^n-1)=v_2(x-1)+v_2(x+1)+v_2(n)-1$ for odd $x$ and even $n$

theorem(s).name: Quadratic_Reciprocity_Supplement_for_2
theorem(s).role: evaluation of $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)$ from $r\bmod 8$

theorem(s).name: Euler_Criterion
theorem(s).role: $x^{(r-1)/2}\equiv \left(\frac{x}{r}\right)\pmod r$

theorem(s).name: Order_Lifting_Lemma_mod_prime_square
theorem(s).role: if $\operatorname{ord}_r(x)=d$ and $x^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$ then $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(x)=rd$

theorem(s).name: Kummer_Theorem_for_Binomial_Valuations
theorem(s).role: $v_\ell\binom{n}{k}$ equals the number of carries in base $\ell$ when adding $k$ and $n-k$

theorem(s).name: Legendre_Formula_for_v_p_factorials
theorem(s).role: $v_p(n!)=(n-s_p(n))/(p-1)$ and $v_p(n!)=\sum_i\lfloor n/p^i\rfloor$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Units modulo $n$ form a finite multiplicative group and the period is a multiplicative order, coupled to valuations by lifting principles.

critical_trick(s): Strengthen the safe-prime component to a prime-square modulus $r^2$ and force $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(a)$ via an order-lifting lemma.
Couple the forced global order $t$ to binomial valuations $v_p\binom{2t}{t}$ and $v_2\binom{2t}{t}$ using Kummer (carry counting) and Legendre (digit-sum) formulas.
Use LTE (odd and 2-adic) to compute $v_2(a^t-1)$ and $v_p(a^t-1)$ from forced local data.

title: Canonical lift of sqrt(-1) to (pq)^4 and multiplicative order modulo 1000

identity.short_name: Canonical lift of sqrt(-1) to (pq)^4 and multiplicative order modulo 1000

problem_spec.summary: Fix canonical square roots of -1 modulo two primes, assemble them via CRT to u mod N=pq, uniquely lift inside the class u mod N to w^2 \equiv -1 mod N^4, reduce w modulo 1000, then compute the least k with t^k \equiv 1 mod 1000 using 1000=8 \cdot 125 and binomial truncation modulo 125.
givens: $p=17$ and $q=37$ are primes
$N=pq$
$a$ is the least positive integer with $a^2\equiv -1\pmod p$
$b$ is the least positive integer with $b^2\equiv -1\pmod q$
$u$ is the unique integer with $0\le u<N$, $u\equiv a\pmod p$, and $u\equiv b\pmod q$
$w$ is the unique integer with $0<w<N^4$, $w\equiv u\pmod N$, and $w^2\equiv -1\pmod{N^4}$
$t$ is the unique integer with $0\le t<1000$ and $t\equiv w\pmod{1000}$
$k$ is the smallest positive integer such that $t^k\equiv 1\pmod{1000}$
ask: Determine $k$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: ensures $x^2\equiv-1\pmod p$ is solvable and has exactly two roots

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor of $N=pq$

parameter(s).name: lift_depth
parameter(s).purpose: target modulus exponent for lifting, from $N$ to $N^4$

parameter(s).name: order_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: mixed prime-power modulus $2^3\cdot 5^3$ for the final order computation

invariant(s).name: two_roots_mod_prime
invariant(s).statement: For each prime $r\in\{p,q\}$, the congruence $x^2\equiv -1\pmod r$ has exactly two solutions $\pm x_0$, so the least positive root is uniquely defined.

invariant(s).name: CRT_unique_u
invariant(s).statement: CRT gives a unique residue class $u\pmod N$ with $u\equiv a\pmod p$ and $u\equiv b\pmod q$, and $0\le u<N$ selects a unique integer $u$.

invariant(s).name: invertible_derivative_condition
invariant(s).statement: From $u^2\equiv -1\pmod N$ we get $\gcd(u,N)=1$, hence $\gcd(2u,N)=1$ since $N$ is odd.

invariant(s).name: iterative_hensel_linearization
invariant(s).statement: Each lift from $N^k$ to $N^{k+1}$ for $x^2+1$ is governed by a forced linear congruence $n+2xt\equiv0\pmod N$, uniquely solvable when $\gcd(2x,N)=1$.

invariant(s).name: mixed_prime_power_order_decomposition
invariant(s).statement: If $\gcd(t,1000)=1$ then $\operatorname{ord}_{1000}(t)=\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_8(t),\operatorname{ord}_{125}(t))$ because $1000=8\cdot125$ and $\gcd(8,125)=1$.

invariant(s).name: fifth_power_binomial_reduction_mod_125
invariant(s).statement: For all integers $m$, $(1+5m)^5\equiv 1+25m\pmod{125}$; this enables controlled exponentiation modulo $125$.

essential_assumption(s): $p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $p\equiv q\equiv 1\pmod4$
The constraint $w\equiv u\pmod N$ is imposed to make the lift to $N^4$ unique
The resulting residue $t\equiv w\pmod{1000}$ is a unit modulo $1000$ so the least $k$ with $t^k\equiv 1\pmod{1000}$ exists
uniqueness_certificate(s): Least positive choice fixes the sign ambiguity in each congruence $x^2\equiv-1\pmod p$ and $x^2\equiv-1\pmod q$, forcing unique $a$ and $b$.
CRT plus the bound $0\le u<N$ forces a unique $u$.

Three successive unique lift corrections (for $N\to N^2\to N^3\to N^4$) force a unique class modulo $N^4$ with $w\equiv u\pmod N$ and $w^2\equiv-1\pmod{N^4}$; restricting $0<w<N^4$ selects a unique $w$.
The normalized residue $t\in[0,999]$ is unique, and the smallest $k$ with $t^k\equiv 1\pmod{1000}$ is unique by minimality.
Order decomposition for coprime moduli ($1000=8\cdot125$) makes $k$ uniquely determined from the uniquely computed component orders.

technique(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_to_root_minus_one
technique(s).purpose: construct a root of $x^2\equiv-1\pmod r$ as $A\cdot B^{-1}$

technique(s).name: CRT_combination
technique(s).purpose: combine local residues into a unique $u$ modulo $N=pq$

technique(s).name: iterated_linear_lift
technique(s).purpose: lift uniquely to a solution modulo $N^{k+1}$ by solving a forced linear congruence modulo $N$

technique(s).name: extended_euclidean_algorithm
technique(s).purpose: compute modular inverses used in CRT and lifting

technique(s).name: multiplicative_order_via_CRT
technique(s).purpose: compute the least $k$ with $t^k\equiv 1\pmod{1000}$ as an lcm of component orders

technique(s).name: binomial_expansion_mod_5_power
technique(s).purpose: evaluate fifth powers modulo $125$ via binomial truncation
outputs: efficient determination of the order modulo $125$

technique(s).name: canonical_residue_normalization
technique(s).purpose: output the unique residue $t\in[0,999]$

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: guarantees unique solutions modulo products of coprime moduli; also supports order decomposition

theorem(s).name: root_counting_in_field
theorem(s).role: a degree-2 polynomial over a field has at most two roots; used for uniqueness of $a,b$

theorem(s).name: Euler_theorem
theorem(s).role: ensures existence of some exponent giving $1$ modulo $m$ for units; supports existence of $k$

theorem(s).name: binomial_theorem
theorem(s).role: enables truncations such as $(1+5m)^5\equiv 1+25m\pmod{125}$

theorem(s).name: order_lcm_for_coprime_moduli
theorem(s).role: establishes the lcm formula for orders when the modulus factors are coprime

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Expand $(x+tN^k)^2+1$ and force divisibility by $N^{k+1}$ (linearization).
Use CRT compatibility to combine modular conditions and to combine orders.

critical_trick(s): Canonicalize signs by least positive roots modulo $p$ and $q$.
Lift uniquely three times to reach modulus $N^4$ using a forced linear congruence each time.
Exploit the mixed prime-power factorization $1000=8\cdot125$ to compute the order via lcm.
Use binomial truncation to compute powers modulo $125$ cleanly.
