task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks aligned to the intended structure:
- Enumerated all residues modulo $11^2$ and counted those whose reduction mod $11$ has order $5$ and for which $11\mid(x^5-1)$ but $11^2\nmid(x^5-1)$; obtained $40$ and lifted by a factor $11^3$ to get $N_{11}=53240$.
- Enumerated all residues modulo $19^2$ and counted those whose reduction mod $19$ has order $18$ and for which $19\mid(x^9+1)$ but $19^2\nmid(x^9+1)$; obtained $108$ and lifted by a factor $19^3$ to get $N_{19}=740772$.
- Multiplied the independent prime-power counts (CRT interface) to get $|S|=39438701280$, hence $|S|\bmod 1000=280$.
Preflight attempts:
Attempt A: Naive heuristic (ignore exact-depth filters and only count order conditions with arbitrary lifts) gives $\varphi(5)\,11^4\cdot\varphi(18)\,19^4\equiv 264\pmod{1000}$, which differs from $280$.
Attempt B: Bounded scan over $x\in\{1,2,\dots,200000\}$ viewed as distinct residues modulo $M$ found $19785$ hits, i.e. $785\bmod 1000$, not $280$.
Attempt C: Partial information (e.g. only knowing $|S|\equiv 30\pmod{125}$) leaves $8$ candidates in $\{0,\dots,999\}$ (namely $30,155,280,405,530,655,780,905$), so it cannot determine the output without the full structure.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent verification via valuation identities:
- "Random stress test on residues modulo $11^5$: for sampled $x$ satisfying the order-$5$ condition mod $11$, verified directly (by checking congruences mod $11^k$) that $v_{11}(x^n-1)=v_{11}(x^5-1)+v_{11}(n/5)$."
- "Random stress test on residues modulo $19^5$: for sampled $x$ satisfying the order-$18$ condition mod $19$, verified directly that $v_{19}(x^n+1)=v_{19}(x^9+1)+v_{19}(n/9)$ (using the odd-exponent sum LTE regime)."
- "Verified the \"unique bad lift property computationally: for each order-$5$ residue mod $11$, among its $11$ lifts to mod $11^2$ exactly one satisfies $y^5\\equiv 1\\pmod{11^2}$; for each order-$18$ residue mod $19$, among its $19$ lifts to mod $19^2$ exactly one satisfies $y^9\\equiv -1\\pmod{19^2}$."

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalized the parent and daughter kernel polynomials in sympy by expand+together+cancel+factor and monic normalization, and attempted equivalence under a deterministic symmetry set:
- swap the two prime components (p<->q),
- substitute $z\mapsto -z$,
- substitute $z\mapsto 1/z$ with denominator clearing,
- substitute $z\mapsto -1/z$ with denominator clearing.
No transform made the parent kernel multiset match the daughter's (the daughter includes a sum-of-powers constraint $z^{d_q}+1$ whereas the parent uses only $z^d-1$ constraints).

verification_notes: Enumerated the prime-square layers: 40 admissible classes modulo 11^2 and 108 modulo 19^2.
Each prime-square class lifts freely to the fifth power modulus, so CRT gives |S|=39438701280.

title: Teichmueller×principal-unit order lift; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y) mod 1000

identity.short_name: Teichmueller×principal-unit order lift; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y) mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Given x,y with equal p-adic valuation and a unit ratio r modulo p^{k+2} that splits as a Teichmueller d-torsion unit times a nontrivial principal unit, compute t=ord_{p^{k+2}}(r) and then evaluate v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y), finally reduced mod 1000.
givens: p,q are distinct odd primes; k>=1; d divides (p-1) with d>=3.
w is a unit modulo p^{k+2} of exact multiplicative order d.
a:=v_p(x)=v_p(y); write x=p^a u and y=p^a v with p∤uv.
Define r in (Z/p^{k+2}Z)^× by r \equiv u v^{-1} (mod p^{k+2}).
Teichmueller/principal structure: r \equiv w (mod p) and s:=r*w^{-1} satisfies s \equiv 1 (mod p^k) but s \neq 1 (mod p^{k+1}).
At the prime q, v_q(x) \neq v_q(y).
ask: Let t := ord_{p^{k+2}}(r). Compute N := v_p(x^t-y^t) + v_q(x-y).
Output the unique integer N0 in {0,...,999} such that N0 \equiv N (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime for the p-adic valuation and modulus p^{k+2}

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: secondary valuation via the unequal-valuation min rule

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the principal-unit perturbation (s \equiv 1 mod p^k but not mod p^{k+1})

parameter(s).name: d
parameter(s).purpose: Teichmueller (root-of-unity) order component

parameter(s).name: w
parameter(s).domain: unit modulo p^{k+2} with ord = d
parameter(s).purpose: fixed Teichmueller component

invariant(s).name: equal_p_valuation
invariant(s).statement: v_p(x)=v_p(y)=a and x=p^a u, y=p^a v with p∤uv.

invariant(s).name: teichmueller_times_principal_unit
invariant(s).statement: r \equiv w (mod p), and s:=r*w^{-1} satisfies s \equiv 1 (mod p^k) but s \neq 1 (mod p^{k+1}).

invariant(s).name: unequal_q_valuations
invariant(s).statement: v_q(x) \neq v_q(y), hence v_q(x-y)=min(v_q(x),v_q(y)).

essential_assumption(s): w has exact order d modulo p^{k+2}.
s:=r*w^{-1} is a nontrivial principal unit at depth k (forces ord(s)=p^2 modulo p^{k+2}).
p∤uv so LTE applies to (u^d, v^d).
v_q(x) \neq v_q(y so the q-adic term is rigid by the min rule.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Mixed-order rigidity: the Teichmueller part has order d and the principal-unit part has order p^2; since the group is abelian and gcd(d,p)=1, the product has unique order t=d*p^2.
Valuation rigidity: LTE fixes v_p(u^t-v^t)=k+2 and the unequal-valuation rule fixes v_q(x-y), so N is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: coprime_order_lcm
technique(s).purpose: combine coprime order components (d and p^2) in an abelian unit group

technique(s).name: vp_product
technique(s).purpose: factor out p^{a t} from x^t-y^t

technique(s).name: compare_vp_on_both_sides
technique(s).purpose: transfer p-adic information between r^d-1 and u^d-v^d

technique(s).name: LTE_minus
technique(s).purpose: evaluate v_p((u^d)^{p^2}-(v^d)^{p^2})

technique(s).name: vp_sum_exact_when_unequal_at_q
technique(s).purpose: compute v_q(x-y) when v_q(x)\neqv_q(y)

technique(s).name: assemble_N
technique(s).purpose: assemble the final integer N before normalization

technique(s).name: mod_m_normalization
technique(s).purpose: map the integer output to {0,...,999} via mod 1000

theorem(s).name: teichmueller_unit_decomposition
theorem(s).role: a unit modulo p^{k+2} splits as (Teichmueller d-torsion)×(principal unit); used to isolate s=r*w^{-1}\equiv1 (mod p).

theorem(s).name: principal_unit_order_mod_p_kplus2
theorem(s).role: if s\equiv1 (mod p^k) but s\neq1 (mod p^{k+1}), then ord_{p^{k+2}}(s)=p^2.

theorem(s).name: exact_division_arguments
theorem(s).role: compare valuations in identities like A=B+p^{k+2}M to obtain exact v_p(A)=v_p(B) when the two terms have unequal p-adic orders.

theorem(s).name: LTE_for_u_n_minus_v_n
theorem(s).role: for odd p|U-V and p∤UV, v_p(U^n-V^n)=v_p(U-V)+v_p(n).

theorem(s).name: min_valuation_rule_for_sum_or_difference
theorem(s).role: if v_q(A)\neqv_q(B), then v_q(A±B)=min(v_q(A),v_q(B)).

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: order decomposition in unit groups
p-adic valuation as a divisibility invariant

critical_trick(s): mixed-order forcing: r = (Teichmueller d-torsion)×(nontrivial principal unit), giving t=d*p^2
transfer-and-LTE: move from r^d-1 to u^d-v^d, then apply LTE at exponent p^2

task(s).reason: Compute x,y,a,u,v,r and t=ord_{p^{k+2}}(r) by factoring phi(p^{k+2}); compute v_p(u^t-v^t) via modular exponentiation and assemble N. Attempt A: naive guess t=d*p gives a different residue (here 632) than the true output. Attempt B: bounded search for n<=1000 with r^nquiv1 (mod p^{k+2}) finds none, so brute force does not shortcut. Attempt C: using only the weaker modulus p^{k+1} yields multiple candidates for N0 in {0,...,999} (here 632 and 105), so partial information does not determine the output.

task(s).reason:
Independently confirm t via the split r=ws: verify ord(w)=d, compute s=r*w^{-1} with v_p(s-1)=k and s
eq1 (mod p^{k+1}), infer ord(s)=p^2, and combine coprime orders to get t=d*p^2. Then compute v_p(u^t-v^t) using LTE on U=u^d, V=v^d and compute v_q(x-y) via the min rule.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit

verification_notes: Computed r=u*v^(-1) mod 29^3 with ord_29^3(w)=7 and ord_29^3(r)=5887.
Exact congruence-depth checks give v29(u^t-v^t)=3 and v13(x-y)=6, so the normalized answer is 105.

title: Count triples with v2(a+b+c)\geqv2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+5 up to 25

identity.short_name: Count triples with v2(a+b+c)\geqv2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+5 up to 25

subdomain(s): valuation behavior under sums vs products

problem_spec.summary: For strictly increasing triples bounded by N, define 2-adic valuation statistics on product and sum; since product valuation additivity makes W=D automatic, count triples where the sum valuation exceeds the weight by a fixed gap.
givens: Positive integers a<b<c with 1\leqa<b<c\leq25.
v2(n) is the 2-adic valuation of n.
Parking weight W=v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c).
Parking depth D=v2(abc).
Parking sum S=v2(a+b+c).
Fixed gap parameter g=5.
A triple is parking-ultraharmonic if W=D and S \geq W+g.
ask: Count parking-ultraharmonic triples (a,b,c) with 1\leqa<b<c\leq25.

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: upper bound for the triple (here 25)

parameter(s).name: prime_p
parameter(s).purpose: p-adic valuation base (here p=2)

parameter(s).name: strict_order
parameter(s).purpose: enforces increasing triples (no repetition)

parameter(s).name: valuation_gap
parameter(s).purpose: strengthens the sum-valuation requirement to S \geq W + g (here g=5)

invariant(s).name: product_valuation_additivity
invariant(s).statement: D=v2(abc)=v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)=W, so W=D always holds.

invariant(s).name: reduced_condition_with_gap
invariant(s).statement: Parking-ultraharmonic reduces to v2(a+b+c) \geq v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+g.

invariant(s).name: sum_valuation_upper_bound_from_size
invariant(s).statement: Since a+b+c \leq N+(N-1)+(N-2)=3N-3, we have v2(a+b+c) \leq floor(log2(3N-3)).

invariant(s).name: parity_feasibility_for_large_gap
invariant(s).statement: If v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)=0 then a,b,c are odd and a+b+c is odd, so v2(a+b+c)=0; thus any requirement v2(a+b+c)\geqg with g\geq1 forces at least one even term.

essential_assumption(s): v2(0) never occurs since a+b+c>0 and a,b,c>0.
Strict ordering a<b<c is part of the counting domain.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Since W=D is automatic by valuation additivity on products, the count is uniquely determined by the single inequality v2(a+b+c) \geq v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+g.
For fixed N and large g, the upper bound v2(a+b+c) \leq floor(log2(3N-3)) forces W to be very small, reducing the problem to finitely many parity/valuation patterns and a finite residue/size check.

technique(s).name: eliminate_redundant_constraint
technique(s).purpose: show W=D always, reducing the definition

technique(s).name: bound_sum_valuation_by_size
technique(s).purpose: bound v2(a+b+c) from above using magnitude

technique(s).name: split_by_parity_pattern
inputs: parity / small v2 patterns of a,b,c
technique(s).purpose: determine which valuation patterns can satisfy a large gap
outputs: W forced to be 1 in the instance (N=25,g=5)

technique(s).name: convert_high_v2_to_congruence_and_exact_sum
technique(s).purpose: turn a high 2-adic valuation into a modular condition and then an exact sum equality

technique(s).name: count_by_even_choice_and_odd_pair_sum
inputs: unique even element e with v2(e)=1 and sum constraint
technique(s).purpose: enumerate admissible e and count odd pairs with fixed sum within bounds
outputs: explicit list of solutions and per-case counts

technique(s).name: sum_case_counts
technique(s).purpose: produce final count

theorem(s).name: p-adic valuation additivity on products
theorem(s).role: v2(abc)=v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)

theorem(s).name: valuation upper bound from size
theorem(s).role: if 0<n<2^{k+1} then v2(n)\leqk (applied to n=a+b+c)

theorem(s).name: valuation of sum via congruences
theorem(s).role: v2(t)\geqk iff t\equiv0 (mod 2^k), used to deduce a+b+c\equiv0 (mod 64)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: 2-adic valuation reduces divisibility conditions to congruences modulo powers of 2.

critical_trick(s): Recognize W=D is automatic, so only the strengthened sum-valuation inequality matters.
Use the size bound a+b+c\leq72 to force v2(a+b+c)\leq6; with gap 5 this forces W=1 and v2(a+b+c)=6.
Convert v2(a+b+c)=6 in the small range to the exact equation a+b+c=64, then do a short finite enumeration of admissible parity patterns.

library(s).reason: brute-force enumerate triples and verify the valuation-gap inequality

task(s).name: brute_force_verify_count
task(s).reason: enumerate all triples 1\leqa<b<c\leqN, compute v2 values, and count those with v2(a+b+c)\geqv2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+g

task(s).name: solution_list_extraction
task(s).reason: print all solutions to confirm the analytic classification

verification_notes: Brute-force checked all C(25,3)=2300 increasing triples and found exactly 3 solutions.

title: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors

identity.short_name: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors

problem_spec.summary: Construct a structured integer $E$ that is coprime to a prescribed set of small primes (e.g., $E=n!\pm 1$ or $E=a^n-b^n$), and output the least prime divisor of $E$, whose size/location certifies a "new prime" phenomenon (and optionally yields prime-gap/compositeness witnesses).
givens: A construction mode is chosen, producing an integer $E>1$ from integer parameters.
Typical modes: (i) factorial: $E=n!\pm 1$; (ii) prime-gap witness: $E=n!+k$ with $2\le k\le n$; (iii) exponential: $E=a^n-b^n$ with $a>b\ge 1$.
ask: Let $p_{\min}$ be the smallest prime divisor of $E$. Determine $p_{\min}$ (as an integer).

parameter(s).name: mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects a Euclid-style construction that forces nontrivial prime divisors

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: controls the factorial scale and the set of excluded small primes

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: selects a particular shifted factorial term $n!+k$

parameter(s).name: a,b
parameter(s).purpose: controls exponential construction $a^n-b^n$ and primitive divisor behavior

invariant(s).name: Euclid_coprime_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The construction guarantees $\gcd(E,\prod_{p\le n}p)=1$ in factorial modes (and analogous "new prime" exclusion in exponential modes), forcing a prime divisor outside the excluded set.

invariant(s).name: least_prime_divisor_is_unique
invariant(s).statement: Every integer $E>1$ has a unique smallest prime divisor $p_{\min}$.

essential_assumption(s): The construction mode must ensure $E>1$ (e.g., $n\ge2$, $a>b\ge1$).
When the instance wants a strict "new prime > n" guarantee, it must use a factorial mode where $E$ is coprime to all integers $2,3,\dots,n$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The smallest prime divisor $p_{\min}$ of a fixed integer $E>1$ is unique by the well-ordering principle on the set of prime divisors.

technique(s).name: Euclid_style_construction
technique(s).purpose: build $E$ so that all "old" primes are excluded as divisors
outputs: a coprimality statement implying existence of a new prime divisor

technique(s).name: factorial_trick_n_factorial_plus_minus_1
technique(s).purpose: ensure any prime divisor of $n!\pm 1$ is $>n$
outputs: lower bound on the least prime divisor

technique(s).name: prime_gap_witness_n_factorial_plus_k
technique(s).purpose: produce explicit composite numbers $n!+k$ and engineer intervals without primes/prime powers

technique(s).name: primitive_prime_divisor_patterns
technique(s).purpose: force existence of a prime divisor not appearing at earlier exponents (Zsigmondy-style)
outputs: a prime $p$ with controlled order properties

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: permits talking about the least prime divisor and uniqueness of prime factorization

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: If an integer is coprime to a large set of smaller integers, any prime divisor must be genuinely new and hence large.

critical_trick(s): Show $E$ is not divisible by any prime in a prescribed set (typically all primes $\le n$) by reducing $E$ modulo those primes.
Conclude existence of a prime divisor outside the set; take the smallest one to make the output canonical.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Core integer arithmetic (factorials, exact products).

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Prime generation (primerange) and primality testing (isprime) for verification scans.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Constructed A=998!+997, B=997!+1, C=2^997-1, and E=A*B*C; verified E>1 and isprime(997).
- "Verified Step 3 computationally: for every prime r<=998, if r|A then r|997."
- Verified that no prime <=997 divides B and no prime <=997 divides C.
NT5 preflight attempts:
- "Small-mod-only attack using moduli {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11}: E gave nonzero residues for all."
- Bounded prime scans up to 900 found no divisor.
- "Answer-collision check: a plausible wrong guess 991 does not divide E."

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent check:
- Scanned primes in increasing order up to 1000 against E; the first divisor found was 997, confirming p_min=997.

verification_notes: 997 divides 998!+997, hence divides E.
No prime below 997 divides E: smaller primes miss 997 in the first factor and exceed the least possible divisors of the other two factors.

title: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $5\le p$ and $2p<q<5p$. Define $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ and set the structured modulus $N=p^k q$. Let $E=(q-1)!+1$, let $p_{\min}$ be the least prime divisor of $E$, and let $r$ be the unique integer with $0\le r<N$ and $r\equiv E\pmod N$. Your final output is the residue of $r$ modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $5\le p$ and $2p<q<5p$.
Define $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ and $N=p^k q$.
Define $E=(q-1)!+1$ and let $p_{\min}$ be the least prime divisor of $E$.
Let $r$ be the unique integer with $0\le r<N$ and $r\equiv E\pmod N$.
ask: (Existence/uniqueness) Prove that $p_{\min}=q$, and deduce that $r$ satisfies the coupled congruences $r\equiv 0\pmod q$ and $r\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$; conclude that such an $r$ is uniquely determined modulo $N$.
(Computation) Determine $r\bmod 1000$ (i.e., report the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ congruent to $r$ modulo $1000$).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: controls the prime-power part $p^k$ of the modulus via the valuation $k=v_p((q-1)!)$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: sets the factorial $(q-1)!$ and (via Wilson/Euclid) forces the least prime divisor of $(q-1)!+1$

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_range_and_distinctness
invariant(s).statement: The constraints $p\ge5$ and $2p<q<5p$ force $q>p$ (hence $\gcd(p,q)=1$) and ensure the valuation exponent $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ is nontrivial (in particular $k\ge2$).

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_prime_power_structure
invariant(s).statement: Because $p\ge5$ implies $5p\le p^2$ and $q<5p$, we have $q<p^2$; hence $v_p((q-1)!)=\lfloor\tfrac{q-1}{p}\rfloor$ and therefore $2\le k\le 4$. In particular, $N=p^k q$ contains a genuine prime-power factor $p^k$ with $k\ge2$ (so $N$ is not squarefree).

invariant(s).name: Euclid_Wilson_and_valuation_congruences
invariant(s).statement: For $E=(q-1)!+1$, Wilson's theorem gives $E\equiv0\pmod q$, and the definition of $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ gives $E\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$.

invariant(s).name: least_prime_divisor_identification
invariant(s).statement: The least prime divisor $p_{\min}$ of $E$ equals $q$: any prime $\ell<q$ divides $(q-1)!$ and hence cannot divide $(q-1)!+1$, while $q\mid (q-1)!+1$ by Wilson.

essential_assumption(s): The exponent $k$ is not an independent knob: it is defined by the valuation $k=v_p((q-1)!)$. Any valid solution must first determine $k$ (VP regime) before solving the Bezout/extended-gcd linear congruence $q\,t\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$ to reconstruct $r$ (LIN regime).

The range guard $2p<q<5p$ is essential: it forces $k\ge2$ and (via $q<p^2$) collapses Legendre's formula to a single nonzero term, preventing degenerate $k\in\{0,1\}$ and ruling out a one-line CRT template with an arbitrary exponent.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Given $(p,q)$, the valuation uniquely fixes $k$ and hence $N=p^k q$. Since $\gcd(p^k,q)=1$, the system $r\equiv0\pmod q$, $r\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$ has a unique solution modulo $N$; imposing $0\le r<N$ makes $r$ unique. Finally, the required normalization reports this unique $r$ reduced modulo $1000$, yielding a unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: Bezout_linear_congruence_glue_step
technique(s).purpose: Interaction kernel (VP->LIN): after $k$ is obtained from the valuation, solve $q\,t\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$ via the extended Euclidean algorithm to reconstruct the unique $r$ with $r\equiv0\pmod q$ and $r\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$.
outputs: an explicit residue $r=q\cdot(q^{-1}\bmod p^k)$ modulo $N=p^k q$, hence $r\bmod1000$

technique(s).name: Wilson_factorial_congruence_mod_q
technique(s).purpose: Certify $q\mid((q-1)!+1)$ so that $E\equiv0\pmod q$.

technique(s).name: Euclid_style_small_prime_exclusion
technique(s).purpose: Exclude every prime $\ell<q$ from dividing $E$ by reducing $E$ modulo $\ell$.
outputs: identification of the least prime divisor $p_{\min}$ as $q$

technique(s).name: Legendre_valuation_of_factorial
technique(s).purpose: Compute $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ (simplified to $\lfloor\tfrac{q-1}{p}\rfloor$ under $q<p^2$) to pin down the exact prime power $p^k$ in $N=p^k q$.
outputs: the valuation-defined exponent $k$ and the congruence $E\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: permits talking about the least prime divisor and guarantees its uniqueness

theorem(s).name: Wilson_theorem
theorem(s).role: gives $(q-1)!\equiv-1\pmod q$, hence $q\mid((q-1)!+1)$

theorem(s).name: Legendre_formula_for_v_p_of_factorial
theorem(s).role: computes $v_p((q-1)!)$; under $q<p^2$ this reduces to a single floor term

theorem(s).name: Bezout_identity_and_extended_gcd
theorem(s).role: produces the modular inverse of $q$ modulo $p^k$, enabling reconstruction of $r$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Couple congruence information from factorial identities with an exact $p$-adic valuation to determine the modulus, then solve a linear congruence to reconstruct the unique residue class.

critical_trick(s): Use Wilson's theorem to force $q\mid (q-1)!+1$ and then exclude all primes $<q$ by the Euclid factorial argument, so $p_{\min}=q$.
Use the constraint $2p<q<5p$ to compute $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ explicitly and ensure $k\ge2$, so the modulus includes a genuine prime power $p^k$.
Interaction step: after $k$ is fixed (VP), solve $q\,t\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$ by extended gcd (LIN) and take $r=q t$; finally reduce $r$ modulo $1000$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Core integer arithmetic (gcd) and safe modular multiplication for factorial residues.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness:
- Recompute $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ by Legendre's floor-sum and confirm $k=\lfloor\frac{q-1}{p}\rfloor$ under $q<p^2$.
- Reconstruct $r$ via $r=q\cdot(q^{-1}\bmod p^k)$ and verify $r\equiv0\pmod q$ and $r\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$.
- Verify $p_{\min}=q$ by checking $E\bmod q=0$ and $E\bmod \ell=1$ for every prime $\ell<q$.
NT5 preflight attempts (cheap strategies that must fail):
- "Small-mod-only attack: compute $E\\bmod m$ for $m\\in\\{2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11\\}$ (here all are $1$), then (incorrectly) force $x\\equiv E\\pmod m$ for all these $m$ and search $x\\in\\{0,1,\\dots,999\\}$; this naive lift returns $x=001$, not the true answer."
- "Tiny bounded scan: search $t\\le 50\\,000$ for $q t\\equiv1\\pmod{p^k}$; no solution appears (the true inverse is much larger), so brute scanning does not reveal $r$ quickly."
- "Template guesses checked and rejected: $r\\bmod1000\\in\\{0,1,q\\bmod1000,(q-1)!+1\\bmod1000\\}$."
NT6 collision resistance:
- Force a plausible wrong branch by replacing $k$ with $k-1$ and recompute the resulting residue modulo $1000$; confirm it differs from the true output.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independently compute $E=(q-1)!+1$ modulo $N=p^k q$ by direct modular factorial multiplication (no CRT / inverse reconstruction), and confirm it matches the reconstructed $r$; compare both modulo $1000$.

verification_notes: Wilson gives 96!+1 ≡ 0 mod 97, and 23^4 divides 96! so 96!+1 ≡ 1 mod 23^4.
CRT reconstruction gives r=20428394 modulo 23^4*97.

title: Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure)

identity.short_name: Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure)

problem_spec.summary: Given a size bound $N$, determine an integer $n\le N$ that optimizes a divisor-structure objective (typically maximizing the number of divisors, or forcing $n$ to be a prime power/squarefree/etc.), reported with a deterministic tie-break rule (e.g., smallest such $n$).
givens: $N\in\mathbb{N},\ N\ge 2$
Objective is fixed for the family (default: maximize divisor count among $1\le n\le N$, with tie-break to the smallest maximizer).
ask: Find $n_*\in\{1,2,\dots,N\}$ maximizing the chosen objective, and if there are multiple, output the smallest such $n_*$.

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: sets a finite search universe and makes extremal arguments meaningful

parameter(s).name: objective_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects a coherent divisor-structure target within the same prime-exponent framework

invariant(s).name: nonincreasing_exponent_shape
invariant(s).statement: For extremal divisor objectives under a size cap, the prime exponents in $n_* = \prod p_i^{e_i}$ typically satisfy $e_1\ge e_2\ge\cdots$ (otherwise one can swap exponents to reduce $n$ without decreasing the objective).

invariant(s).name: prime_packing_heuristic
invariant(s).statement: Extremal solutions prioritize small primes first: if a large prime $q$ divides $n_*$, replacing $q$ by a smaller prime (when feasible) improves size efficiency for the same exponent budget.

essential_assumption(s): A deterministic tie-break rule (e.g., "choose the smallest maximizer") is part of the family definition and is required for uniqueness.
In "smoothness_constraints_light" mode, the smoothness bound is an explicit parameter so the decision problem stays decidable.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Because the search universe $\{1,\dots,N\}$ is finite, an optimum exists. The tie-break rule selects exactly one integer, making the output globally unique.

technique(s).name: prime_exponent_vectors
technique(s).purpose: translate divisor-structure objectives into constraints on exponent vectors
outputs: optimization problem on integer exponent vectors

technique(s).name: extremal_divisor_choice
technique(s).purpose: choose an extremal object and perform local modifications to derive structural constraints

technique(s).name: minimal_prime_factor_choice
inputs: a counterexample extremal with an avoidable large prime factor
technique(s).purpose: replace a large prime factor by a smaller one while preserving objective to get contradiction
outputs: proof that only small primes appear in extremal candidates

technique(s).name: inequality_bounds_for_integers
technique(s).purpose: convert factorization constraints into numeric bounds on exponents
outputs: finite enumeration range for feasible exponent vectors

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: justify that prime exponent vectors parameterize all $n$ uniquely

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers are uniquely determined by their prime exponents; optimizing an arithmetic property often reduces to optimizing over exponent vectors under a size constraint.

critical_trick(s): Convert the objective to constraints on the prime exponent vector and show exponents must be nonincreasing with primes in increasing order.
Use a local replacement argument (swap primes/exponents) to rule out non-optimal shapes, then enumerate the remaining finite candidates.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Core integer arithmetic for bounds and products.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: Lightweight enumeration of exponent vectors / bounded searches.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Reliable prime factorization (factorint) and exact divisor-count computation for verification.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
(1) Enumerate feasible nonincreasing exponent vectors over primes 2,3,5,7 with product <= N; reconstruct n and compute tau(n); confirm smallest argmax is 840.
(2) Confirm no n <= N has tau(n) >= 33 by direct search and by verifying that the minimal n with tau(n) >= 36 already exceeds N.
NT5 preflight attempts:
(a) Mod-heuristics: checking only divisibility/residues modulo m in {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11} does not isolate the extremal candidate (many n <= N share each such residue pattern).
(b) Bounded scan: restricting to n <= 800 yields best = 720, so partial scanning fails.
(c) Template guesses (largest power of 2/3/5; largest factorial <= N; largest multiple of 30/60/120) do not produce the answer.
NT6 collision resistance:
Verify the plausible wrong-branch guess 720 differs from 840.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Brute force over all n=1..N computing tau(n) directly from prime factorizations and selecting the smallest maximizer; confirm it matches 840.

verification_notes: Recursive search over nonincreasing prime exponents found the smallest maximizer n*=840.
It attains tau(n*)=32 among all n≤973.

title: Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure) - Realized Instance

identity.short_name: Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure) - Realized Instance

problem_spec.summary:
Let $\tau(n)$ be the number of positive divisors of $n$. For a given bound $N$, define $n_*$ to be the smallest integer in $\{1,2,\dots,N\}$ for which $\tau(n)$ is maximal on $\{1,2,\dots,N\}$. From the odd-prime structure of $n_*$, build a composite modulus of the forced form $M=p^kq$ (odd primes $p\ne q$, $k\ge2$) and consider the linear congruence $n_*x\equiv n_*\pmod M$.
The task is to count, modulo $1000$, how many residue classes $x\pmod M$ solve this congruence while also satisfying exact $p$-adic divisibility and a $q$-avoidance condition on $x-1$.
givens: $N\in\mathbb{N}$ with $N\ge 60$.
Let $\tau(n)$ denote the number of positive divisors of $n$.
Define
$\displaystyle n_*\;=\;\min\Bigl\{n\in\{1,2,\dots,N\}:\ \tau(n)=\max_{1\le m\le N}\tau(m)\Bigr\}$.
Assume (and hence restrict to admissible instances where) $n_*$ has at least two distinct odd prime divisors.
Let $p$ be the smallest odd prime dividing $n_*$ and $q$ the second smallest odd prime dividing $n_*$. Let $a=v_p(n_*)$.
Define the structured modulus $M=p^{2a}q$ (so $M$ has the form $p^kq$ with $k=2a\ge2$ and $\gcd(p,q)=1$).
Let $S\subset \mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z}$ be the set of residue classes $x\pmod M$ satisfying simultaneously:
$n_*x\equiv n_*\pmod M$,
ask: Let $C=|S|$. Determine $C\bmod 1000$ (report the final answer reduced to an integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$).

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: sets a nontrivial extremal divisor landscape so $n_*$ is forced by prime-exponent reasoning and the derived modulus/valuation constraints are meaningful

invariant(s).name: nonincreasing_exponent_shape
invariant(s).statement: For the extremal divisor objective under a size cap, writing $n_*=\prod p_i^{e_i}$ with $p_1<p_2<\cdots$, one has $e_1\ge e_2\ge\cdots$ (otherwise swapping exponents yields a smaller integer with at least as many divisors, contradicting minimality of $n_*$ among maximizers).

invariant(s).name: prime_packing_heuristic
invariant(s).statement: Extremal solutions prioritize small primes first: if a large prime $r$ divides an extremal candidate, replacing $r$ by a smaller prime (when feasible under the size bound) improves size efficiency for the same or larger divisor count.

invariant(s).name: odd_prime_divisors_guard
invariant(s).statement: Admissible instances are restricted to those where $n_*$ has at least two distinct odd prime divisors; this guarantees that the derived primes $p<q$ exist and are uniquely defined.

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus_prime_power
invariant(s).statement: With $a=v_p(n_*)\ge1$ and $p,q$ distinct odd primes, the constructed modulus $M=p^{2a}q$ has a genuine prime-power component ($2a\ge2$) and is neither prime nor squarefree.

invariant(s).name: gcd_reduction_kernel
invariant(s).statement: Because $M$ has only prime factors $p$ and $q$ with $v_p(M)=2a$ and $v_q(M)=1$, and $v_p(n_*)=a$ and $v_q(n_*)\ge1$, one has $\gcd(n_*,M)=p^a q$ and therefore $n_*x\equiv n_*\pmod M$ is equivalent to $x\equiv 1\pmod{p^a}$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_filter_count_is_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Under the congruence reduction $x\equiv 1\pmod{p^a}$, the simultaneous conditions $v_p(x-1)=a$ and $v_q(x-1)=0$ select exactly $p^{a-1}(p-1)(q-1)$ residue classes modulo $M$ (hence $S$ is nonempty and its size is forced by $p,q,a$).

essential_assumption(s): A deterministic tie-break rule (choose the smallest maximizer) is part of the family definition and is required for $n_*$ to be uniquely defined.
Instances are restricted so that $n_*$ has at least two distinct odd prime divisors, ensuring the derived primes $p<q$ and the modulus $M=p^{2a}q$ are well-defined and non-degenerate.

The intended solution path is structural (prime-exponent extremal reduction to control $n_*$, then gcd/Bezout reduction of a linear congruence, then exact $p$-adic filtering and $q$-avoidance counting). One-step template solutions by brute-force search over $1\le n\le N$ or over all $x\pmod M$ are excluded.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The extremal definition plus the deterministic tie-break makes $n_*$ a unique function of $N$. The derived quantities $p,q,a,M$ are then uniquely determined, and the set $S\subset\mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z}$ is well-defined. Hence $C=|S|$ is uniquely determined; the reported answer is the canonical representative of $C$ modulo $1000$, an unambiguous integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: prime_exponent_vectors
technique(s).purpose: translate the divisor-count objective into constraints on exponent vectors under the size cap $n\le N$
outputs: optimization problem on integer exponent vectors; monotone exponent-shape constraints

technique(s).name: extremal_divisor_choice
technique(s).purpose: choose an extremal object and perform local modifications to derive structural constraints

technique(s).name: minimal_prime_factor_choice
inputs: a counterexample extremal with an avoidable large prime factor
technique(s).purpose: replace a large prime factor by a smaller one while preserving or improving the divisor objective to get contradiction
outputs: proof that only sufficiently small primes appear in extremal candidates

technique(s).name: inequality_bounds_for_integers
technique(s).purpose: convert factorization constraints into explicit bounds on exponents, yielding a finite candidate list
outputs: finite enumeration range for feasible exponent vectors

technique(s).name: linear_congruence_via_Bezout
technique(s).purpose: reduce solvability and the full solution set to gcd data via Bezout, then parametrize all solutions as a residue class modulo $M/\gcd(A,M)$
outputs: explicit description of all solutions to $n_*x\equiv n_*\pmod M$

technique(s).name: p_adic_valuation_filtering
inputs: exact divisibility constraints such as $p^t\parallel (x-1)$ and a coprimality condition $q\nmid(x-1)$
technique(s).purpose: count solutions inside an arithmetic progression by translating to conditions on a parameter and using $p$-adic valuations together with coprimality at a second prime
outputs: closed-form count for $|S|$ in terms of $p,q,a$

technique(s).name: interaction_linear_congruence_plus_p_adic_filters
technique(s).purpose: the kernel interaction step: turn the linear congruence into $x\equiv 1\pmod{p^a}$, then count the admissible lifts modulo $M=p^{2a}q$ using exact $p$-adic valuation and exclusion of the residue class $x\equiv1\pmod q$

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: justify that prime exponent vectors parameterize all $n$ uniquely and that $\tau(n)$ depends multiplicatively on exponents

theorem(s).name: linear_congruence_solution_count
theorem(s).role: guarantee that $A x\equiv B\pmod M$ is solvable iff $\gcd(A,M)\mid B$, and describe/count solutions via reduction modulo $M/\gcd(A,M)$

theorem(s).name: basic_p_adic_valuation_properties
theorem(s).role: justify exact-divisibility statements ($p^t\parallel m$) and how they translate into counting constraints on parameters in arithmetic progressions

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers are uniquely determined by prime exponents; extremal arithmetic objectives reduce to exponent-vector constraints, and linear congruences reduce to gcd/Bezout structure whose solution sets can then be filtered by $p$-adic divisibility.

critical_trick(s): Convert the extremal divisor objective into constraints on the prime exponent vector of $n_*$ (nonincreasing exponents, small-prime packing), yielding a sharply restricted candidate class for $n_*.$
Reduce $n_*x\equiv n_*\pmod M$ to $x\equiv 1\pmod{p^a}$ via gcd/Bezout, then count admissible residue classes using the exact condition $p^a\parallel(x-1)$ together with $q\nmid(x-1)$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: gcd/lcm computations and modular arithmetic for verification scaffolding

library(s).name: array
library(s).reason: memory-efficient integer arrays for the SPF sieve up to N=7,000,000

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: independent factorization cross-check (factorint) and sanity confirmation of prime-power structure

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Enumerated all nonincreasing prime-exponent vectors with product <= N to compute max_{m<=N} tau(m) and the smallest maximizer n_*.
- Computed (p,q,a), the modulus M=p^{2a}q, and C via the closed form p^{a-1}(p-1)(q-1).
NT5 preflight attempts (cheap-strategy rejection):
- "small-mod-only attack (mods 2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11): with L=lcm=27720, N=60 and N=27780 are congruent modulo every listed modulus, but give different outputs (A=8 vs A=24), so the output is not determined by these residues."
- "bounded-scan heuristic: N=4000000 gives A=24, whereas this instance N=7000000 gives A=216, so extrapolating from large-but-smaller ranges fails."
- "template fits on 300 random N in [60,10^6]: pure periodic models of period <=6 fail (explicit counterexamples found for every period 1..6), and no affine mod-1000 model nor affine+period(k<=6) model fits the sampled data."
Collision resistance:
- wrong-branch guess using n=4324320 (one record earlier) yields A_wrong=72 != 216 (mod 1000).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent verification:
- Computed tau(n) for all 1<=n<=N via a smallest-prime-factor sieve and confirmed the same n_* and max tau.
- Brute-enumerated all x mod M and counted those satisfying (i)-(iii); count equals 216.

final_answer.normalization: A is the representative in {0,...,999} of C mod 1000.

verification_notes: Recursive search found n*=6486480 with tau(n*)=400; then p=3, q=5, a=4.
Direct count modulo M=32805 gives |S|=216.

title: Gen-3 capstone: reciprocity-coupled slice count modulo $p^3$

identity.short_name: Gen-3 capstone: reciprocity-coupled slice count modulo $p^3$

subdomain(s): sums of two squares and Gaussian integers

problem_spec.summary: Compute the number of pairs $(x,y)\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^3\mathbb{Z})^2$ on the slice $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ satisfying the shifted norm congruence $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$, where $s$ is coupled to a representation $p=a^2+b^2$ with $a=b+1$ and the sign $T=(\frac{q}{p})$. Output the count modulo $999$.
givens: Primes $p$ and $q$ satisfy $q\equiv 3\pmod 4$ and $q<p$.
Integers $a>b>0$ satisfy $a^2+b^2=p$ and $a=b+1$.
Let $T=(\frac{q}{p})$ and let $s$ be the unique integer $0\le s<p$ with $s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p$.
Define $S=\{(x,y)\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^3\mathbb{Z})^2 : x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3},\ x\equiv sy\pmod p\}$ and $N=|S|$.
Let $A$ be the unique integer $0\le A<999$ with $A\equiv N\pmod{999}$.
ask: Determine $A$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).domain: odd prime with a representation $p=m^2+(m+1)^2$ for some integer $m\ge 2$
parameter(s).purpose: forces the exact identity $(a+b)^2+1=2p$ used in the reduction modulo $p^3$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the sign $T=(\frac{q}{p})$

parameter(s).name: lift_level
parameter(s).purpose: forces a second-stage lift in the slice parameter $k$ from modulo $p$ to modulo $p^2$

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: normalizes the final answer as a residue modulo $999$

invariant(s).name: consecutive_identity
invariant(s).statement: With $a=b+1$ and $u=a+b$, one has the exact identity $u^2+1=2p$ and $0<u<p$.

invariant(s).name: coupled_slice_parameter
invariant(s).statement: The residue $s$ is the unique integer $0\le s<p$ with $s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_filter
invariant(s).statement: Any solution of $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ satisfies $p\nmid y$.

invariant(s).name: reduction_mod_p2
invariant(s).statement: On writing $x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^3}$, the condition $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ is equivalent to $2y^2+2syk+pk^2\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$.

invariant(s).name: hensel_unique_k
invariant(s).statement: For each $y\pmod{p^2}$ with $p\nmid y$, the congruence $2y^2+2syk+pk^2\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$ has a unique solution $k\pmod{p^2}$.

essential_assumption(s): The coupling $s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p$ links the quadratic-residue sign $T$ with the norm representation.
The modulus is $p^3$, so counting requires lifting $k$ from modulo $p$ to modulo $p^2$.
The shifted norm level $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ forces the valuation filter $p\nmid y$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): With $p,q$ fixed, $T=(\frac{q}{p})$ and $(a,b)$ are fixed, hence $s$ is fixed. The valuation filter forces $p\nmid y$. For each $y\pmod{p^2}$ with $p\nmid y$ there is a unique $k\pmod{p^2}$ solving the reduced congruence, and each such $y\pmod{p^2}$ has exactly $p$ lifts to $y\pmod{p^3}$. This forces $N=p^2(p-1)$ in the $T=1$ branch and $N=0$ in the $T=-1$ branch, hence the normalized residue $A$ is unique.

technique(s).name: norm_representation_with_consecutive_constraint
technique(s).purpose: compute $(a,b)$ and the identity $(a+b)^2+1=2p$

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: compute the sign $T$

technique(s).name: linear_slice_parametrization
technique(s).purpose: write $x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^3}$ with $k\pmod{p^2}$

technique(s).name: p_adic_valuation_case_split

title: Order modulo product of safe primes via reciprocity split and CRT-lcm

identity.short_name: Order modulo product of safe primes via reciprocity split and CRT-lcm

problem_spec.summary: Given two safe primes p1=2r1+1 and p2=2r2+1 and an odd prime q simultaneously constrained by q|(p1-2) and q|(p2+2), quadratic reciprocity reduces (q/p1) and (q/p2) to the supplementary evaluations of (-2/q) and (2/q), which via Euler's criterion force the local orders ord_{p1}(q) and ord_{p2}(q). The global order modulo n=p1 p2 is then forced by a CRT-lcm composition argument, and the output is the reduction modulo 1000.
givens: Let p1,p2 be odd primes such that r1=(p1-1)/2 and r2=(p2-1)/2 are also odd primes (so p1,p2 are safe primes).
Let q be an odd prime such that q|(p1-2) and q|(p2+2).
Let n=p1 p2 and define k=ord_n(q), the least positive integer with q^k \equiv 1 (mod n).
ask: (i) Using quadratic reciprocity together with p1 \equiv 2 (mod q) and p2 \equiv -2 (mod q), reduce (q/p1) and (q/p2) to explicit evaluations of (-2/q) and (2/q) (hence to conditions on q mod 8).
(ii) Use Euler's criterion and the safe-prime structure to determine k1=ord_{p1}(q) and k2=ord_{p2}(q).
(iii) Use the Chinese remainder theorem to prove k=ord_{p1 p2}(q)=lcm(k1,k2) and report the normalized answer N defined by 0 \leq N < 1000 and N \equiv k (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: p1
parameter(s).domain: odd prime p1 such that r1=(p1-1)/2 is an odd prime (safe prime)
parameter(s).purpose: first prime modulus; its safe-prime factorization forces a sharp local order dichotomy

parameter(s).name: p2
parameter(s).domain: odd prime p2 such that r2=(p2-1)/2 is an odd prime (safe prime), with p2 != p1
parameter(s).purpose: second prime modulus; provides a second local order dichotomy whose combination is nontrivial

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime q with q|(p1-2) and q|(p2+2)
parameter(s).purpose: forces two distinct reciprocity collapses: (q/p1)\to(-2/q) and (q/p2)\to(2/q)

invariant(s).name: safe_prime_local_order_sieve
invariant(s).statement: With p_i=2r_i+1 and r_i prime, (Z/p_i Z)^× is cyclic of order 2r_i, so any element order lies in {1,2,r_i,2r_i}; excluding q \equiv ±1 (mod p_i) yields ord_{p_i}(q) \in {r_i,2r_i}.

invariant(s).name: reciprocity_split_via_pm2
invariant(s).statement: Because p1 \equiv 2 (mod q) and p2 \equiv -2 (mod q) with p1 \equiv p2 \equiv 3 (mod 4), quadratic reciprocity gives (q/p1)=(-1/q)(2/q)=(-2/q) and (q/p2)=(-1/q)(-2/q)=(2/q), reducing both Legendre symbols to supplementary evaluations from q mod 8.

invariant(s).name: Euler_character_to_local_order
invariant(s).statement: Euler's criterion yields q^{r_i} \equiv (q/p_i) (mod p_i), so (q/p_i)=1 forces ord_{p_i}(q)=r_i while (q/p_i)=-1 forces ord_{p_i}(q)=2r_i under the safe-prime local sieve.

invariant(s).name: CRT_lcm_global_order
invariant(s).statement: For n=p1 p2 with gcd(p1,p2)=1, the Chinese remainder theorem implies q^t \equiv 1 (mod n) iff q^t \equiv 1 (mod p1) and (mod p2); hence ord_n(q)=lcm(ord_{p1}(q),ord_{p2}(q)).

essential_assumption(s): Prime-mode: p1,p2 are distinct safe primes so that each local unit group has order 2r_i with r_i prime, enabling a two-branch local order classification.

Link is structural: the coupled congruences q|(p1-2) and q|(p2+2) are used to force a reciprocity split into (-2/q) and (2/q); removing either link breaks deterministic computation of both local quadratic characters.

Nontriviality guard: since 1<q<p_i, we automatically avoid q \equiv ±1 (mod p_i), preventing local orders 1 or 2 and forcing the argument to pass through quadratic-character evaluation and CRT composition.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Local uniqueness: safe-prime structure plus the computed Legendre symbol (q/p_i) \in {±1} forces a unique local order ord_{p_i}(q) \in {r_i,2r_i} via Euler's criterion.
Global uniqueness: CRT implies ord_{p1 p2}(q)=lcm(ord_{p1}(q),ord_{p2}(q)), uniquely determining k and hence uniquely determining N \equiv k (mod 1000) with 0 \leq N < 1000.

technique(s).name: safe_prime_order_dichotomy
technique(s).purpose: reduce each local multiplicative order to the two candidates r_i or 2r_i after excluding trivial residues

technique(s).name: multiplicative_order
inputs: unit group modulo a prime or coprime modulus
technique(s).purpose: formalize k=ord_m(a) and derive divisibility constraints from group order
outputs: divisor restrictions and minimality properties for orders

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: swap (q/p_i) to (p_i/q) with the correct sign, then use the forced congruences p1 \equiv 2 (mod q) and p2 \equiv -2 (mod q)

technique(s).name: supplementary_laws
technique(s).purpose: evaluate (2/q) and (-2/q) via q mod 8 to finish the reciprocity computation

technique(s).name: Euler_criterion
inputs: odd prime modulus p and unit q
technique(s).purpose: bridge quadratic character to exponent: q^{(p-1)/2} \equiv (q/p) (mod p)
outputs: forced local order choice under the safe-prime dichotomy

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
technique(s).purpose: translate congruences modulo p1 p2 into simultaneous congruences modulo p1 and p2
outputs: CRT equivalence used to compose local order information

technique(s).name: CRT_order_lcm
technique(s).purpose: use CRT plus order minimality to prove ord_{p1 p2}(q)=lcm(k1,k2) (new kernel step)
outputs: global order k in closed form

technique(s).name: mod_m_normalization
technique(s).purpose: produce the required normalized output N with 0 \leq N < 1000 and N \equiv k (mod 1000)

theorem(s).name: cyclicity_of_Fp_star
theorem(s).role: ensures (Z/pZ)^× is cyclic, so element orders divide p-1 and are controlled by the prime factorization of p-1

theorem(s).name: fermat_little_theorem
theorem(s).role: guarantees existence of multiplicative orders by a^{p-1} \equiv 1 (mod p) for p ∤ a

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: identifies Z/(p1 p2)Z with Z/p1Z × Z/p2Z, enabling the lcm-of-orders certificate

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Quadratic characters split the unit group into squares and nonsquares; Euler's criterion converts this split into a forced exponent.
For coprime moduli, congruences modulo the product are equivalent to simultaneous congruences; orders compose via an lcm constraint.

critical_trick(s): Use the coupled constraints p1 \equiv 2 (mod q) and p2 \equiv -2 (mod q) to force a reciprocity split: (q/p1)\to(-2/q) and (q/p2)\to(2/q).
Exploit the safe-prime structure so each local order is in {r_i,2r_i} and Euler's criterion selects the correct branch.
New kernel: a CRT-driven lcm certificate to pass from local orders to the global order modulo p1 p2.
Finish by the mandated reduction modulo 1000.

title: Quadratic residue decision via Legendre/Jacobi and reciprocity

identity.short_name: Quadratic residue decision via Legendre/Jacobi and reciprocity

problem_spec.summary: Given an odd prime $p$ (or an odd modulus $n$) and an integer $a$, decide quadratic solvability $x^2\equiv a$ modulo the modulus by computing the Legendre/Jacobi symbol using Euler's criterion and quadratic reciprocity (including supplementary laws).
givens: $a\in\mathbb{Z}$
Either: $p$ is an odd prime; or: $n$ is an odd positive integer (Jacobi-symbol mode).
ask: Output $\chi\in\{-1,0,1\}$ equal to the Legendre symbol $(a/p)$ in prime mode, or the Jacobi symbol $(a/n)$ in composite mode, thereby deciding solvability when applicable.

parameter(s).name: modulus_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects Legendre vs. Jacobi setting

parameter(s).name: p_or_n
parameter(s).domain: odd integer; prime in prime_p mode
parameter(s).purpose: the modulus

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: the residue whose quadratic character is queried

invariant(s).name: Euler_criterion_invariant
invariant(s).statement: For odd prime $p$ with $p\nmid a$, $(a/p)\equiv a^{(p-1)/2}\pmod p$ (Euler criterion), giving a computational route to the quadratic character.

invariant(s).name: reciprocity_reduction
invariant(s).statement: Quadratic reciprocity and supplementary laws reduce $(a/p)$ to symbols with smaller arguments, ultimately to $(\pm 1/p)$ and $(2/p)$.

essential_assumption(s): Prime mode requires $p$ to be an odd prime for Legendre symbol properties and Euler criterion to apply.

In Jacobi mode, the Jacobi symbol may be $1$ even when $a$ is a nonresidue modulo $n$; instances that use Jacobi mode must be clear whether they seek only a necessary condition or a full solvability decision.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The Legendre/Jacobi symbol value $\chi\in\{-1,0,1\}$ is uniquely determined by $(a,\text{modulus})$; reciprocity provides a deterministic reduction algorithm, yielding a unique output.

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: swap numerator/denominator in Legendre symbols to reduce computation size
outputs: recurrence reducing symbol evaluation to smaller cases

technique(s).name: supplementary_laws
technique(s).purpose: evaluate $(\!-1/p)$ and $(2/p)$ using $p\bmod 4$ and $p\bmod 8$ patterns
outputs: explicit symbol values from small congruence classes

technique(s).name: Euler_criterion
technique(s).purpose: compute $(a/p)$ via modular exponentiation

technique(s).name: mod_p_obstructions_for_Diophantine
technique(s).purpose: rule out integer solutions by detecting a quadratic nonresidue obstruction modulo $p$
outputs: proof of impossibility via local obstruction

theorem(s).name: quadratic_residue_mod_p
theorem(s).role: frames the solvability question $x^2\equiv a\pmod p$ as a residue/nonresidue classification

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Squares modulo primes occupy a structured subset; their membership can be tested by a character (Legendre symbol) computable via reciprocity and modular exponentiation.

critical_trick(s): Reduce symbol computation using reciprocity until only $(\!-1/p)$ and $(2/p)$ remain, which are read off from $p\bmod 4$ and $p\bmod 8$.
Translate residue decisions into Diophantine obstructions by reducing equations modulo $p$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: core integer arithmetic (gcd, modular exponentiation scaffolding)

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness + NT5 preflight attempts.

Correctness checks:
- Verified p is an odd prime and gcd(a,p)=1.
- Computed chi by Euler criterion pow(a,(p-1)//2,p) and by sympy.legendre_symbol; they agree.

NT5 preflight attempts (all fail to predict chi correctly):
- Small-mod-only attack using mods {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11}: since a is a nonsquare modulo 3 and 7, a naive predictor guesses chi=-1 (wrong).
- Tiny bounded scan x=0..2000 for x^2quiv a (mod p): found no solution and guessed chi=-1 (wrong).
- Template guess using only p mod 8 (guess chi=(2/p)): predicts -1 (wrong).

NT6 collision:
- Wrong-branch normalized output (chi=-1 -> 999) differs from the true normalized output (chi=1 -> 001).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independent brute check: enumerated all residues x^2 mod p (0<=x<p) and confirmed a mod p lies in the set; equivalently found square roots via sympy.sqrt_mod.

verification_notes: Euler criterion gives chi=1 for a mod p.
An explicit square root witness is x=76305 modulo 185893, so the normalized output is 1.

title: Quadratic residue decision via Legendre/Jacobi and reciprocity - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Quadratic residue decision via Legendre/Jacobi and reciprocity - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ be a safe prime (so $p=2r+1$ with $r$ prime), and let $q$ be an odd prime divisor of $p-2$. The congruence $p\equiv 2\pmod q$ forces a quadratic-reciprocity reduction of $(q/p)$ to the supplementary evaluation of $(-2/q)$, and Euler's criterion then ties that quadratic character to a rigid order dichotomy in $(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times$. The task is to determine $k=\operatorname{ord}_p(q)$ and report $k\bmod 1000$.
givens: Let $p$ be an odd prime such that $r=(p-1)/2$ is also an odd prime (so $p\ge 11$ and $|\,(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times\,|=2r$).
Let $q$ be an odd prime divisor of $p-2$ (so $p\equiv 2\pmod q$ and automatically $1<q<p$).
Define $k=\operatorname{ord}_p(q)$, the least positive integer such that $q^k\equiv 1\pmod p$.
ask: (i) Let $\varepsilon=(q/p)\in\{\pm1\}$ be the Legendre symbol. Using quadratic reciprocity together with $p\equiv 2\pmod q$, reduce $\varepsilon$ to an explicit evaluation of $(-2/q)$ (hence to a condition on $q\bmod 8$), and prove that this forces $k\in\{r,2r\}$.
(ii) Determine $k$ from $\varepsilon$ and report the final answer as the integer $k\bmod 1000$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).domain: odd prime $p\ge 11$ such that $r=(p-1)/2$ is an odd prime
parameter(s).purpose: safe-prime modulus so $(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times$ has order $2r$ with $r$ prime, enabling a sharp order dichotomy after a quadratic-character test

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: forces a reciprocity swap (core QR step) and then feeds the resulting quadratic character into an order determination (ORDER step)

invariant(s).name: anti_trivial_safe_prime_guard
invariant(s).statement: With $r=(p-1)/2$ an odd prime and $p\ge 11$, one has $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$ and $|\,(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times\,|=2r$ where $r$ is prime; hence any element order divides $2r$ and can only be $1,2,r,$ or $2r$.

invariant(s).name: anti_trivial_link_and_noncollapse
invariant(s).statement: Since $q$ is an odd prime divisor of $p-2$, we have $p\equiv 2\pmod q$ and $1<q<p$. In particular $q\not\equiv \pm 1\pmod p$, so $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)\notin\{1,2\}$.

invariant(s).name: reciprocity_forced_reduction
invariant(s).statement: Because $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$ and $p\equiv 2\pmod q$, quadratic reciprocity yields $(q/p)=(-1)^{((p-1)/2)((q-1)/2)}(p/q)=(-1/q)\,(2/q)=(-2/q)$, reducing the QR decision to the supplementary laws for $(-1/q)$ and $(2/q)$ (equivalently $q\bmod 8$).

invariant(s).name: order_dichotomy_from_character
invariant(s).statement: Let $r=(p-1)/2$. Euler's criterion gives $q^r\equiv(q/p)\pmod p$. If $(q/p)=1$ then $q^r\equiv 1$ so $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)\mid r$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)=r$ by the noncollapse guard. If $(q/p)=-1$ then $q^r\equiv -1$, so $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)\nmid r$ and the only remaining possibility is $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)=2r$.

essential_assumption(s): Prime-mode only: $p$ is an odd prime and $r=(p-1)/2$ is an odd prime, so $(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times$ has the rigid divisor structure needed for an order dichotomy.

Anti-template guard: the instance is constructed so that determining $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)$ cannot be resolved by the trivial cases $q\equiv\pm1\pmod p$; the intended determination must pass through (a) a reciprocity-based evaluation of $(q/p)$ forced by $q\mid(p-2)$, then (b) order-divisor reasoning inside $(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times$.

The congruence link $q\mid(p-2)$ is structural (not cosmetic): it forces the reciprocity swap $(q/p)\to(p/q)$ to collapse to $(2/q)$, and removing it breaks the deterministic reduction that pins down the quadratic character needed to select the correct order.
uniqueness_certificate(s): For each admissible instance $(p,q)$, the multiplicative order $k=\operatorname{ord}_p(q)$ is uniquely defined as a least positive exponent. The safe-prime invariant forces $k\in\{r,2r\}$ after excluding $\{1,2\}$, and the reciprocity-forced reduction computes $(q/p)\in\{\pm1\}$ deterministically (via $(-2/q)$), which selects the correct element of $\{r,2r\}$. The reported output is then uniquely determined as $k\bmod 1000$ by the normalization rule.

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: compute $(q/p)$ by swapping to $(p/q)$ with the correct sign; here the extra congruence $p\equiv 2\pmod q$ makes the swap collapse to a small-symbol evaluation

technique(s).name: supplementary_laws
technique(s).purpose: evaluate $(-1/q)$ and $(2/q)$ from $q\bmod 4$ and $q\bmod 8$, thereby finishing the reciprocity reduction of $(q/p)=(-2/q)$

technique(s).name: Euler_criterion
technique(s).purpose: bridge quadratic character to an exponent: $q^{(p-1)/2}\equiv(q/p)\pmod p$

technique(s).name: QR_to_order_bridge
inputs: the value $(q/p)$ and the factorization $p-1=2r$ with $r$ prime
technique(s).purpose: interaction step (QR + ORDER): use the quadratic character to decide whether $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)$ divides $(p-1)/2$ or equals $p-1$, converting reciprocity output into an exact multiplicative order

technique(s).name: mod_p_obstructions_for_Diophantine
technique(s).purpose: optional extension pathway: interpret a nonresidue outcome as a local obstruction to congruence solvability (retained for lineage continuity)
outputs: proof of impossibility via local obstruction when applicable

theorem(s).name: quadratic_residue_mod_p
theorem(s).role: frames $(q/p)$ as the residue/nonresidue classifier for $x^2\equiv q\pmod p$ and supplies the square-subgroup viewpoint used in the order argument

theorem(s).name: cyclicity_of_Fp_star
theorem(s).role: ensures $(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times$ is cyclic of order $p-1$, so element orders divide $p-1$ and are controlled by its prime factorization

theorem(s).name: fermat_little_theorem
theorem(s).role: guarantees $q^{p-1}\equiv 1\pmod p$ for $p\nmid q$, anchoring the order definition

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Quadratic characters partition $(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times$ into squares and nonsquares; membership in the square subgroup forces order to divide $(p-1)/2$.
In a finite cyclic group, knowing one nontrivial power (here $q^r\equiv\pm1$) and the prime-factor structure of the group order can force the exact order.

critical_trick(s): Use $q\mid(p-2)$ to force the reciprocity swap $(q/p)\to(p/q)$ and collapse it to the supplementary evaluation $(-2/q)$.
Exploit the safe-prime structure $p-1=2r$ (with $r$ prime) so that $\operatorname{ord}_p(q)\in\{r,2r\}$ after excluding $\{1,2\}$, then select via the computed quadratic character.
Finalize with the mandated normalization $k\bmod 1000$.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks (this instance):
- Verified p=199379 is prime and r=(p-1)/2=99689 is prime.
- Verified q=22153 is prime and q | (p-2).
- "Verified reciprocity identity numerically: legendre_symbol(q,p) == legendre_symbol(-2,q) == 1."
- "Verified Euler criterion output: pow(q,r,p) == 1."
- Verified k is not 1 or 2 (q not congruent to 1 or -1 mod p), and pow(q,r,p)==1.

NT5 preflight attempts (cheap-strategy resistance):
- "Small-mod-only attack: using only residues (p mod m, q mod m) for m in {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11} cannot pin down N in general. Among sampled valid instances with p<=2,000,000 (safe primes) and q chosen as the largest prime factor of p-2, a collision was found: two different instances share the same small-mod signature but yield different N (example: (p,q)=(17327,11) gives N=663, while (p,q)=(72767,11) gives N=383)."
- "Tiny bounded scan: searched for an exponent e<=5000 with q^e \\equiv 1 (mod p) and found none, so the order is not discoverable by a small-range brute force."
- "Template checks: always guessing k=r fails on sampled instances (both k=r and k=2r occur); always guessing k=2r fails on this instance (would give 378 mod 1000 instead of 689). Pure-period predictors based on q mod d for d<=6 fail on sampled instances (each residue class contains multiple N values)."

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independently computed ord_p(q) in two ways:
- sympy.n_order(q,p) returned k=99689.
- divisor test using the only candidates {1,2,r,2r} (since p-1=2r with r prime) showed the minimal exponent giving 1 is r.
Both methods agree and give N = k mod 1000 = 689.

verification_notes: Direct divisor search in (Z/199379Z)^x gives ord_p(q)=99689.
The normalized output is 689.
